fairfax-bos-2026-06-23.digest: The Board of Supervisors unanimously approved multiple development projects including a Tysons conference center, expedited reviews for Enterprise RAC and Sekas Homes projects, rezoning for Pulte Homes to build 158 residential units, and property sales including 2.2 million gallons per day of wastewater treatment capacity to Alexandria. The board also placed two bond measures (parks facilities and community health services) on the November 2026 ballot and extended the deadline for issuing general obligation bonds. Supervisor Patrick S. Herrity abstained on federal grant funding comments and voted alone against purchasing property in the Springfield District.
notable: Tysons conference center and multiple housing projects approved unanimously; major development push forward
Patrick S. Herrity cast sole 'no' vote against Springfield District property purchase
Two bond measures for parks and community health services headed to November ballot

fairfax-bos-2026-06-09.digest: The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors unanimously approved 25 items on June 9, 2026, including rezoning for APB Woodland Park LLC's property development with agreed mitigation conditions, amendments to the comprehensive plan for the RH1 area, and approval of public hearings on several land-use and infrastructure changes—including allowing storage yard use at a recycling center property and permitting higher-density development with a private school on 20 acres currently zoned industrial/commercial. The board also issued several proclamations, approved fiscal year 2026 financial closing, and made routine governance decisions with no dissenting votes.
notable: APB Woodland Park LLC rezoning approved with conditional mitigation requirements for land development
Comprehensive plan amended for RH1 area and private school/higher-density development authorized on 20-acre industrial/commercial site
All 25 items passed unanimously (10-0) with no failed motions or dissent

fairfax-bos-2026-05-19.digest: The board passed 27 routine items with no significant opposition, including appointments, recognitions, and housekeeping measures. Notable decisions were approving the county's five-year community development and housing plan (2027-2031), authorizing an appeal of a tax assessment by Defense Government Contracting, and approving multiple zoning and development applications. One supervisor abstained on recognizing Fairfax County Public Schools, and Supervisor Herrity opposed the tax appeal authorization.
notable: Five-year community development and housing plan approved for 2027-2031
Tax assessment appeal authorized 9-1, with Supervisor Patrick S. Herrity dissenting
Multiple zoning/development applications approved including multifamily residential, Chick-fil-A drive-through modifications, and single-family home construction

fairfax-bos-2026-05-05.digest: The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors passed 49 items with near-unanimous support, including changes to affordable housing developer fees, approval of a joint redevelopment agreement with the City of Fairfax for two community facilities, summer bus service changes, multiple rezonings permitting residential and mixed-use development, and several comprehensive plan amendments. Supervisor Patrick S. Herrity dissented on three separate May 19 proclamations and abstained on the Willard Health Center/Sherwood Community Center redevelopment agreement. Most other business—designations, honors, appointments, and procedural matters—passed unanimously.
notable: Affordable housing developer fee structure changed and multiple rezonings approved to permit residential development at various densities across the county.
Willard Health Center and Sherwood Community Center joint redevelopment with City of Fairfax approved 9–0–1, with Herrity abstaining.
Supervisor Patrick S. Herrity opposed three identical May 19 proclamations and abstained on the facilities agreement, representing the only dissent of the meeting.

fairfax-bos-2026-04-28.digest: The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors approved the current fiscal year budget for the county and schools with Patrick S. Herrity voting no. The board also reviewed and marked up the county executive's proposed fiscal year 2027 budget for the county and schools in three separate actions, with Herrity dissenting each time. Additionally, the board unanimously approved the county capital budget.
notable: FY2027 budget markup passed 9-1 with Supervisor Herrity opposing all three markup votes
Current fiscal year budget approved 9-1 with Herrity's lone dissent
Capital budget approved unanimously 10-0

la-primegov-18005.digest: City Council approved 18 routine matters with unanimous votes (13 ayes, 2 absences). Key actions include: approving five property subdivision and parcel division maps across various streets; authorizing Painting with a Twist to serve alcohol on Devonshire Street; funding dedicated street lighting repairs in Council District 5 and a peer-to-peer training pilot in Council District 3; and transferring the Climate Emergency Mobilization Office from Public Works to Emergency Management. All votes passed without dissent or failed motions.
notable: Street lighting crew and repairs funded for Council District 5.
Climate Emergency Mobilization Office moved from Department of Public Works to Emergency Management Department.
Five property divisions and subdivisions approved across the city.

la-primegov-18324.digest: The City Council passed eight items with minimal attendance, including a water district easement through Chatsworth Parks, approval for animal shelter retail operations to generate revenue, permanent appointment of a new Animal Services General Manager, and a budget transfer to cover Fire Department overtime costs. Five council members were absent for all votes, with a quorum of ten voting yes on each measure and no dissenting votes recorded.
notable: Water pipeline easement granted through Chatsworth Parks for Las Virgenes water district pump station project.
Animal shelters authorized to sell pet supplies and rent retail space to fund shelter operations.
Fire Department received budget transfer to cover overtime and sick time account shortfalls.

la-primegov-18334.digest: City Council passed 30 items unanimously or near-unanimously with three members absent. Key decisions included approving $21.137 million in bonds for two affordable housing projects (41 units at The Lincoln Hotel and 46 units at Oatsie's Place), authorizing a speed camera contract with Verra Mobility, and vacating a portion of Reseda Boulevard. One item drew dissent: Councilmembers Hernandez and Jurado opposed designating a location in Council District 6 for enforcement against sitting and lying in public spaces. The remaining items covered telecommunications contracts, property transfers, cannabis equity provisions, staffing appointments, and various funding allocations for arts, community services, and FIFA World Cup events.
notable: $21.137 million in bonds approved for two affordable housing developments (87 total units)
Speed camera safety system contract authorized with Verra Mobility
Two councilmembers (Hernandez, Jurado) opposed the public-space enforcement designation in CD 6

la-primegov-18364.digest: The City Council passed 57 items with near-unanimous support, including $40 million in bonds for two affordable housing projects in Council District 11, continued funding for a homeless navigation center in CD 4, and parking restrictions in CD 15. Three votes showed dissent: extending the local emergency declaration deadline from 30 to 60 days (4 no), renewing a security contract with ASSI (3 no), and Soto-Martínez and Raman were absent. The council also backed Palisades fire relief via a property tax exemption and opposed a HUD rule on equal access housing protections.
notable: $40 million in bonds approved for two 49-unit and 41-unit affordable housing projects in CD 11.
Local emergency declaration deadline extended to 60 days despite 4 dissenting votes (Hernandez, Jurado, Rodriguez, Soto-Martínez opposed).
Property tax exemption for Palisades fire victims and opposition to HUD's equal access housing rule rescission both passed unanimously.

la-primegov-17723.digest: City Council passed 51 items with five members frequently absent. Major actions included approving the El Corazon Art Park Project at 1st and Broadway despite a union environmental appeal, updating zoning in Boyle Heights and the Ventura/Cahuenga Boulevard corridor, authorizing a mixed-use development at 400 South Central Avenue in Boyle Heights, selling property at 11381 West Chalon Road, and removing 12 rental properties from the rent escrow program after housing violations were resolved. The council also approved multiple Port of Los Angeles permit amendments, designated two historic-cultural monuments, ratified the Mayor's January 2025 wildfire emergency declarations, and hired outside counsel for multiple lawsuits. All items passed unanimously among voting members with no failed motions or dissenting votes recorded.
notable: Boyle Heights zoning update and 400 South Central Avenue mixed-use project approved—significant land use changes for that neighborhood
12 rental properties removed from escrow program after violations were resolved, releasing city-held rental income back to landlords
Five council members absent throughout most of the meeting, with only 10 voting on nearly all items

la-primegov-18006.digest: The City Council passed 33 measures including establishing a special assessment district for streetlight maintenance, granting water pipeline easements across two Chatsworth parks, approving the Housing Department's fiscal 2026-27 budget (with three dissenting votes), and authorizing multiple nonprofit leases for homeless housing and community programs. The council also approved cultural designations for four downtown intersections and a separate intersection honoring community figures, plus water reuse and affordable housing initiatives. Four council members were absent throughout the meeting.
notable: Housing Department budget passed 10-3, with Councilmembers Lee, Park, and Rodriguez voting no
Water pipeline easements granted across Chatsworth parks for Twin Lakes Pump Station Project
Special assessment district created to fund ongoing streetlight maintenance costs

loudoun-bos-2026-01-06.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved 13 items on January 6, including amendments to the FY 2026 Capital Improvement Program, allocation of unassigned General Fund balance to specified purposes, and a directive to increase local tax funding toward capital projects over the next three budgets. One noteworthy split: Supervisor Matthew F. Letourneau dissented on endorsing the Environmental Commission's position for testimony at a Dominion Energy hearing, while the board voted 8–1 to proceed. Supervisor Koran Saines was absent throughout.
notable: Board directs increased local tax funding to capital projects in next three budgets
Environmental Commission's Dominion Energy hearing position endorsed 8–1; Letourneau opposed
Supervisor Koran Saines absent for all votes

loudoun-bos-2026-01-21.digest: The Board of Supervisors directed county staff to allocate more local tax funding to capital improvements with 20 percent dedicated to housing in future budgets, and approved multiple development projects including rezoning for the Pantovic Property (though three supervisors opposed) and Village at Clear Springs. The board also authorized several construction contracts for Crosstrail Boulevard and other infrastructure, affirmed 2026 personal property tax rates, and expressed support for two Virginia House Bills on voting access and public defender funding. Three supervisors were frequently absent during votes, with Kristen Umstattd missing the entire meeting.
notable: Board directed 20% of future capital improvement funds go to housing—opposed by Kershner and Letourneau.
Pantovic Property rezoning passed 4-3, with Saines, Letourneau, and Randall voting no.
Board denied a $30,130 damage claim for dog injuries to a resident.

loudoun-bos-2026-02-03.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved eight items including support for House Bill 395 on portable solar devices and House Bill 1021 on affordable housing and assisted living, both with one supervisor (Matthew F. Letourneau) voting no. The board also adopted guidelines for managing invasive plants on county land, confirmed board appointments, and recognized February as Black History Month. Three supervisors were absent for most votes.
notable: HB 1021 support passed 6-1-1 with Letourneau voting no and Kershner abstaining
HB 395 support passed 7-1 with Letourneau as sole dissent
Board entered closed session to discuss acquiring land for public use

loudoun-bos-2026-02-11.digest: The Board of Supervisors met on February 11, 2026, but the provided record contains no details about motions, votes, decisions, or actions taken. Without specific information about agenda items, resolutions passed, budget allocations, or policy changes, I cannot summarize what the board actually accomplished or decided.

loudoun-bos-2026-02-18.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved over 20 items including $31.75 million in apartment construction loans (Clear Springs, Dogwood Farm Station, and Dulles Plaza), updated employee handbooks and board bylaws, and federal community development grants. A zoning map amendment for the Auto World Substation project passed 5–3, with supervisors Juli Briskman, Laura TeKrony, and Phyllis J. Randall voting against it. Supervisor Koran Saines was absent from all votes.
notable: $31.75 million in housing loans approved across three apartment projects
Auto World Substation zoning amendment split 5–3 with three supervisors opposed (Briskman, TeKrony, Randall voting no)
Supervisor Koran Saines absent from entire meeting

loudoun-bos-2026-03-03.digest: The Board of Supervisors passed ten routine items unanimously (7-0) with two supervisors absent, including proclamations for American Red Cross Month and Groundwater Awareness Week, updates to bylaws for several advisory boards focused on housing and agriculture, board member confirmations, and approval of administrative matters. Notably, the board waived public hearings for a zoning amendment related to proffers, allowing that process to proceed without community input meetings.
notable: Board waived public hearings required for a zoning amendment related to proffers, eliminating community input meetings on that land-use decision.
Two supervisors (Caleb Kershner and Kristen Umstattd) were absent; all nine other items passed 7-0 with no dissent.

loudoun-bos-2026-03-17.digest: The Board of Supervisors unanimously passed 22 items on March 17, 2026, with two supervisors absent. Major actions included awarding construction and security contracts, spending $1.27 million in federal American Rescue Plan funding, approving zoning changes for Franklin Park West development, and directing staff to study roadway options north of Leesburg on Route 15. The board also renewed the public safety radio contract, increased computer purchasing limits, and approved design development for the Western Loudoun Recreation Complex.
notable: Board spent $1.27 million in federal ARPA funds and approved Franklin Park West zoning with no dissent or failed motions
Two supervisors (Matthew F. Letourneau and Sylvia Glass) absent for most votes; Route 15 study passed 8-0 with only Glass absent
All 22 items passed unanimously with seven votes each (or eight when Letourneau was present)

loudoun-bos-2026-04-07.digest: The Board of Supervisors passed routine ceremonial proclamations for April observances (Public Safety Telecommunicators Week, Volunteer Week, Fair Housing Month, Sexual Assault Awareness Month, and Child Abuse Prevention Month) and approved administrative items including updated bylaws for the Transit Advisory Board and Environmental Commission. The board directed staff to study an overnight parking ban on Woodson Drive and to research redevelopment options for the former George Washington University property now owned by Amazon Data Services in Algonkian. The board also allocated $7,000 to Leesburg for a "Follow the Frenchman" documentary project. Supervisor Sylvia Glass was absent from the entire meeting; Supervisor Caleb Kershner was absent for two votes.
notable: Board allocated $7,000 to Town of Leesburg for "Follow the Frenchman" documentary project.
Staff directed to study overnight parking ban (5 p.m.–7 a.m.) on Woodson Drive.
Staff directed to research redevelopment options for Amazon Data Services' Algonkian District property.

loudoun-bos-2026-04-21.digest: The Board of Supervisors passed 33 routine and significant items on April 21, 2026, with three supervisors absent. Major actions included authorizing $165.15 million in bonds for capital projects, renewing multiple service contracts (road construction, playgrounds, adult corrections facility staffing, and natural gas), advancing the Goose Creek Trailhead Park project and a US Route 50 interchange design, and approving a zoning map change for West Belmont development (Kristen Umstattd opposed). The board also adopted cannabis zoning amendments to comply with state law and scheduled two closed sessions for contract and property acquisition discussions. Supervisors Caleb Kershner, Koran Saines, and Sylvia Glass were absent throughout; Kershner abstained on one transportation and land use matter.
notable: Board authorized $165.15 million in bonds for capital projects and amended the FY2026 Capital Improvement Program.
West Belmont zoning map change approved 7-1, with Kristen Umstattd voting no.
Three supervisors absent; Caleb Kershner abstained on a transportation/land use decision.

loudoun-bos-2026-05-04.digest: The Board of Supervisors passed 14 zoning ordinance amendments updating Loudoun County's land-use rules based on Transportation and Land Use Committee recommendations. Most changes passed with 8 votes, though several faced dissent: Supervisor Caleb Kershner opposed six measures, Juli Briskman abstained on a structure-reuse requirement, and Laura TeKrony opposed one amendment. One motion creating tiered standards for large events (200+ people) drew opposition from both Kershner and Supervisor Koran Saines. Supervisor Kristen Umstattd was absent throughout.
notable: Board passed comprehensive zoning updates with one dissenting vote each on six separate ordinances (Caleb Kershner opposed most).
Tiered event standards for 200+ person gatherings split the board 5–2, with Kershner and Saines in opposition.
New requirement that 50% of structures in nonresidential covered activities be reused or repurposed passed 7–1 with Briskman abstaining.

loudoun-bos-2026-05-05.digest: The Board of Supervisors passed routine proclamations, administrative appointments, and key transportation and land-use initiatives. Notable substantive decisions include adopting the Safe Streets Loudoun roadway safety plan, endorsing a Western Loudoun rural roads corridor study (opposed by Supervisor TeKrony), and directing staff to draft a Human Rights Ordinance (opposed by Supervisors Kershner and Letourneau). The Board also reconsidered and remanded the Cochran Tech zoning applications back to the Planning Commission for a public hearing, with Supervisor TeKrony opposing and Supervisor Briskman abstaining.
notable: Human Rights Ordinance directing staff draft passed 7–2, with Kershner and Letourneau opposed.
Cochran Tech zoning applications remanded to Planning Commission after reconsideration; TeKrony opposed, Briskman abstained.
Western Loudoun North-South Rural Roads Corridor Study endorsed 8–1 with TeKrony opposing.

loudoun-bos-2026-05-19.digest: The Board of Supervisors passed 23 items unanimously (8–0, with Supervisor Koran Saines absent), mixing ceremonial proclamations and honors with operational decisions. Key actions include renewing IT and road design contracts, awarding an AT&T equipment deal, approving a compressor station project for Eastern Gas Transmission in Leesburg, naming Dulles South Community Park as Lightridge Park, and scheduling a final Quantum Park zoning decision for October. The board also endorsed speed management plans for two roads and required future planning documents to include electrical substation and power line data.
notable: All 23 items passed unanimously with no dissenting votes or failed motions.
Approved a compressor station project for Eastern Gas Transmission and Storage, Inc. in Leesburg under specified conditions.
Scheduled Quantum Park zoning change application for final Board decision on October 20, 2026.

loudoun-bos-2026-06-02.digest: The Board of Supervisors passed 13 routine items including a zoning ordinance amendment, design guidelines for the Unison Historic District, and approval of various proclamations (Pride Month, Gun Violence Awareness Day, Pollinator Week). Three supervisors were absent. Substantive actions included the historic district project approval and closed sessions to discuss potential property disposition and legal matters.
notable: Zoning ordinance amendment approved as part of consent agenda
Unison Historic District design guidelines and budget authorized
Three supervisors absent for all votes (Kershner, Letourneau, Saines)

loudoun-bos-2026-06-16.digest: The Board of Supervisors passed 25 items in a largely routine meeting, including contract renewals for public safety radio and maintenance services, a capital budget amendment, creation of five new county positions (one in Housing and Community Development, four in a new Security and Intelligence Operations Center), $346,980 in grants to community organizations, and endorsement of two road/trail projects. The board also voted to end commuter bus pilot services from Dale City and Frederick effective September 30, 2026, with Supervisor Matthew F. Letourneau dissenting on one personnel referral motion. Supervisors Caleb Kershner and Koran Saines were absent for most votes.
notable: Board creates new Security and Intelligence Operations Center with four full-time positions and approves separate Housing and Community Development position
Commuter bus pilot services to Dale City and Frederick ending September 30, 2026
Supervisor Matthew F. Letourneau opposed referring eligible applicants to next program review stage (8-1 vote)

loudoun-bos-2026-07-07.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved seven items with unanimous support among present members (7-0), including the Natural Resources Strategy, two housing advisory board reports, and routine administrative approvals. Two supervisors—Caleb Kershner and Koran Saines—were absent. All votes passed without dissent or opposition.

pittsburgh-legistar-12028.digest: City Council unanimously approved $500,000 in federal highway funding for the PJ McArdle Bridge design, reducing vehicle fleet spending by $988,402, and authorizing a $250,000 contract for the Safe Passages youth violence prevention program. Council rejected a proposal to transfer eight vacant lots in the 12th Ward to the Pittsburgh Land Bank but approved transferring vacant lots in the 18th Ward and one lot on Auburn Street in the 12th Ward to the Land Bank at no cost to the city.
notable: Council unanimously rejected transferring eight vacant 12th Ward lots to Pittsburgh Land Bank (9 no) while approving similar transfers of nine other city-owned vacant lots elsewhere.
$500,000 in no-cost federal highway funding added to McArdle Bridge preliminary design project.
Safe Passages youth violence prevention program receives $250,000 one-year contract with Operation Better Block.

pittsburgh-legistar-12022.digest: City Council approved $552.8 million in settlement and lawsuit payouts totaling over $550,000, redirected $31,509 from neighborhood initiatives to slope repairs, and authorized over $1.7 million in engineering contracts for bridge and tunnel projects—all with state reimbursement covering most costs. The council also approved multiple road conversions from concrete to asphalt, facility improvements, and private infrastructure easements, with three council members absent throughout the meeting.
notable: Council paid $550,296.64 across eight separate lawsuits and one employment discrimination settlement with no dissenting votes
Redirected $31,508.90 from neighborhood initiatives funding to slope failure remediation
Authorized preliminary design work for Corliss Tunnel (up to $1M state-funded) and two bridge projects ($763K in engineering contracts, fully reimbursable)

pittsburgh-legistar-12017.digest: City Council approved tax exemptions for new construction and building renovation projects on Pittsburgh's Northside, and established new taxes and licensing rules for arcade games and slot machines. Both measures passed unanimously among the eight council members present, with Barbara Warwick absent.

pittsburgh-legistar-12013.digest: City Council passed 17 measures on a 8-1 vote (with Councilmember Salinetro absent). Key actions include authorizing the sale of 23 tax-acquired properties while canceling 27 incomplete property sales, accepting $800,000 in county funding to expand services for homeless and housing-insecure residents, and approving Passport Academy Charter School's new elementary/secondary school at 1835 Forbes Avenue. The council also established new urban garden and greenways programs on city land, received $25,000 in grant funding for land acquisition and greenway improvements, and approved spending increases totaling $316,590 for copier services and street imagery mapping.
notable: $800,000 county funding approved to expand homeless and housing-insecure services
Passport Academy Charter School approved to operate elementary/secondary school at 1835 Forbes Avenue
New urban garden and greenways programs authorized on city-owned land with $25,000 grant funding received

pittsburgh-legistar-12005.digest: Council unanimously approved over $5.7 million in infrastructure investments, including $1 million for Complete Streets, $1.07 million for street resurfacing, and $2.5 million for the East Ohio Street Safety project (all reimbursable or grant-funded). The body also approved $550,000 reallocating sports facility funds to play area improvements, authorized lead dust testing to enforce lead safety rules, accepted a $10,000 donation for historic preservation, and approved a three-year municipal waste collection contract with Wilkinsburg Borough for $3.4 million.
notable: All 12 items passed 9-0 with no dissent or failed motions.
Largest commitment: $3.4 million three-year waste collection contract with Wilkinsburg Borough.
Major infrastructure: $2.5 million for East Ohio Street Safety project (100% reimbursable from state).

pittsburgh-legistar-12000.digest: The city council unanimously approved 14 measures, including two major state-funded transportation grants ($750,000 increase for Beaver Avenue Esplanade and $1.98 million increase for Liberty Avenue safety improvements, both at no city cost), two park rehabilitation grant applications totaling $287,500 each with $37,500 local matches, and a $103,133 one-year wildlife management agreement for deer control. The council also approved zoning changes permitting retail tobacco, inhalants, and cannabinoid products; spending up to $210,000 for an employment program; $54,950 for Independence Day entertainment; and $394,636 for website software over six years. Councilmember Robert Charland was absent.
notable: State transportation reimbursement for Liberty Avenue increased by $1.98 million (fully state-funded) and Beaver Avenue by $750,000 (fully state-funded)
City approved $103,133 annual deer control contract with USDA and zoning changes to permit retail tobacco, inhalants, and cannabinoid products
All 14 measures passed unanimously with one absent (Robert Charland); no dissent recorded

pittsburgh-legistar-11995.digest: City Council unanimously passed 18 distinct measures (with councilmember Bobby Wilson absent) totaling significant spending and policy changes: $481,320 for emergency snow removal, $1.67 million in EPA grants for yard waste collection improvements, $514,000 total for the 250th Independence Day fireworks (with $261,800 added via grant funding), and $75,000 to extend the Bromberg & Associates communications contract. The council also rezoned Hazelwood parcels from industrial to mixed-use, established new taxes and licensing for mechanical amusement devices, and accepted grants for youth climate solutions, recreation center improvements, and Homewood school redevelopment.
notable: $481,320 emergency snow removal warrant approved
$1.67 million EPA grant accepted for yard waste program overhaul
Hazelwood rezoning from industrial to mixed-use allows more diverse development

pittsburgh-legistar-11985.digest: City Council passed 16 measures in a routine session with Councilmember Bobby Wilson absent. Major approvals include $4 million in PennDOT funding (80% match) for bike-share expansion, $630,000 from the Richard King Mellon Foundation for the city's 250th anniversary celebration, and $125,000 to repave Hazelwood Trail with community partners. The council also authorized street paving on Elwell Street, approved legal services contracts totaling over $40,000, and eliminated the Records Management Advisory Commission. All items passed 8–0 with no dissenting votes.
notable: $4 million secured from PennDOT for bike-share expansion (city plus Bike Share Pittsburgh covering $800K local match)
$630,000 grant approved for the city's 250th anniversary celebration
Records Management Advisory Commission eliminated by repealing its city code chapter

pittsburgh-legistar-11975.digest: City Council unanimously approved $400,000 in state funding for the Carnegie Library's Squirrel Hill branch HVAC and roofing repairs, plus $9.87 million for the City Steps Project (80% state-funded). The council shifted $226,141 in capital funds from parks and recreation to sports facilities, hired a security contractor for $69,000 over five years, and extended software contracts costing $286,740 additional for budgeting tools. A partnership with Johns Hopkins University on data science and a $25,000 state grant for LGBTQ+ historical documentation also passed, all without dissent.
notable: Council unanimously approved $400,000 state grant for Carnegie Library Squirrel Hill branch repairs (HVAC and roofing).
Reallocated $226,141 from park reconstruction and recreation centers to sports facilities.
Approved $9.87 million increase for City Steps Project engineering and construction (80% state-funded).

pittsburgh-legistar-11970.digest: City Council unanimously approved 17 measures including a $6.67 million contract for Smithfield Street reconstruction, increased spending on park improvements totaling nearly $200,000 in additional costs, and a ban on immigration enforcement in city spaces. The council also settled a pending lawsuit for $5,000, accepted a free right-of-way land donation for bridge work, and transferred six vacant properties to the Pittsburgh Land Bank. Councilmember Bobby Wilson was absent; all other eight members voted yes on every item.
notable: Largest expenditure: $6.67 million Smithfield Street reconstruction contract with A. Merante Contracting Inc.
Policy shift: City prohibits immigration enforcement operations in city-owned or operated spaces
No dissenting votes recorded; all 17 items passed unanimously among present members

pittsburgh-legistar-11963.digest: Council unanimously approved spending on professional services contracts and litigation settlements totaling over $214,000, authorized 97 new open-ended vendor agreements worth up to $1.5 million annually each, and reallocated $55,692.60 in federal COVID funds to food banks. No dissenting votes were recorded on any measure.
notable: Council approved 97 new open-ended professional services contracts with maximum annual values up to $1.5 million each, significantly expanding vendor agreements from 151 to 151 total agreements.
Approved $214,119.99 in settlements and legal expenses including $101,256.25 to settle Mahouski litigation and $90,588.24 for police labor arbitration services.
Reallocated $55,692.60 in remaining federal COVID relief funding to two food banks.

pittsburgh-legistar-11956.digest: City Council unanimously approved over $5.8 million in grant funding and matching commitments for parks, trails, and youth sports facilities, including $5 million from University of Pittsburgh and multiple state grants for tree planting, trail improvements, and park renovations. The council also authorized $131,654.50 in legal and arbitration settlements, accepted free land from Pittsburgh Regional Transit for park expansion, and redirected $400,000 from recreation and senior centers to building remediation—with Councilmember Deborah Gross dissenting on the latter redirect. All other routine business including technology contracts and NFL Draft amendments passed unanimously.
notable: Council member Deborah Gross cast the only dissenting vote opposing the $400,000 redirect from recreation and senior centers to condemned building remediation.
The city committed to $1 million in matching funds across multiple state grant applications for parks and trails, leveraging up to $1.05 million in state funding.
Council accepted a $5 million grant from University of Pittsburgh for parks, capital improvements, public safety, and economic development.

princewilliam-bos-2026-07-07.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved a zoning map amendment creating an e-commerce overlay district county-wide, initiated comprehensive plan amendments for properties in Occoquan and Gainesville, and deferred a Savage Estates land-use proposal. A motion to schedule a public hearing on development rights purchase code amendments failed 4–4, with supervisors Bailey, Stewart, Vega, and LaCroix voting no. Supervisor Vega also opposed directing staff to address health determinants in the comprehensive plan per state law, losing 7–1.
notable: E-commerce overlay zoning district approved across the county.
Failed 4–4 vote on scheduling hearing for development rights purchase code amendment; Bailey, Stewart, Vega, and LaCroix opposed.
Vega cast the only no vote on health determinants comprehensive plan directive (7–1 passage).

princewilliam-bos-2026-06-23.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved two major land sales: 3.8 acres on University Boulevard for a 500-bed student residential building and 11.06 acres on Hornbaker Road to Sandberg Development Inc. The board also approved a special use permit for the Vint Hill Substation despite opposition from Supervisors Stewart and LaCroix, adopted a remote meeting policy, and canceled the July 7 evening meeting. Supervisor Vega was absent from all votes.
notable: Land sales totaling ~14.86 acres approved for residential and commercial development
Vint Hill Substation permit passed 5-2 with Stewart and LaCroix voting no
Remote meeting policy adopted, allowing board members to attend electronically

princewilliam-bos-2026-06-09.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved a zoning rule change process for the Downtown Core Overlay District and passed procedural measures allowing remote participation and unscheduled agenda items. Two supervisors abstained on an unspecified directive, and the board held a closed session on confidential matters that was later confirmed as legally conducted. Supervisor Vega was absent for most votes.
notable: Zoning changes initiated for Downtown Core Overlay District
Directive 4 passed with two abstentions (Jefferson and Bailey opposed or declined)
Board members may now participate remotely without standard procedural requirements

stafford-bos-2026-06-16.digest: The Board of Supervisors passed 11 resolutions and ordinances on June 16, 2026, including R26-241, R26-144, R26-124, O26-15, O26-19, O26-24, O26-22, and R26-223, all with unanimous or near-unanimous support. Two items drew dissent: Ordinance O26-22 failed to achieve unanimity with Mr. English and Crystal Vanuch voting no, and an unnamed item also split 5-2 with English and Deuntay Diggs opposing. The board deferred Ordinance O26-25 to August and held a closed session that was later certified.
notable: Split votes on two ordinances: O26-22 opposed by English and Vanuch; unnamed item opposed by English and Diggs (5-2 each)
O26-25 deferred to August meeting rather than decided
Meeting extended past midnight by unanimous board action

stafford-bos-2026-06-02.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved 13 resolutions and one proposal on June 2, 2026, with most votes unanimous among the six present members. Mr. English voted no on proposal P26-10, and Pamela Yeung voted no on Resolution R26-189 while Ms. Allen abstained. The board also held a closed meeting and approved its certification. Crystal Vanuch was absent.
notable: Mr. English dissented on P26-10 (5–1 vote to approve)
Pamela Yeung opposed Resolution R26-189; Ms. Allen abstained (4–1–1 vote to approve)

stafford-bos-2026-05-26.digest: The Board of Supervisors held a closed session on May 26, 2026, with one supervisor absent. The board voted unanimously (6–0) to authorize and certify the closed meeting, though no public details were disclosed about the topics discussed.

fairfax-bos-2026-04-16.digest: The Board of Supervisors continued its public hearing on the FY 2027 budget and five-year capital plan to a later date, with one supervisor voting in favor. No new budget details were discussed or decided at this meeting.

fairfax-bos-2026-04-14.digest: The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors held a full agenda meeting with all 10 supervisors voting unanimously on 31 items, including multiple rezonings and development approvals, fee adjustments for land development services, adoption of the FY2027 budget, and amendments to fire and erosion control codes. Key land-use decisions approved site-specific plan amendments for Fairfax Center and Reston Transit Station areas, rezoning of TPC Freedom properties for mixed-use residential development, and permits for 16 attached dwellings and an adult day care facility. The board also approved summer transit service changes for Fairfax Connector and authorized an opioid litigation settlement, with no dissenting votes on any measure.
notable: FY2027 budget and multiple fee increases for land development, plan review, and permits all approved unanimously
TPC Freedom rezoning from commercial/industrial to planned district for mixed-use development with 1.2 floor area ratio approved; Reston Transit Station Area Plan amendment also approved
No dissenting votes across all 31 agenda items

fairfax-bos-2026-04-07.digest: The Board of Supervisors adopted a policy allowing remote participation in joint meetings between Fairfax County and Fairfax City, passing with 3 ayes. The same measure appears to have been voted on twice in the record provided.
notable: Remote participation now permitted in Fairfax County–Fairfax City interjurisdictional meetings (unanimous 3-0 vote)

fairfax-bos-2026-03-17.digest: The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors held a routine meeting dominated by resolutions, recognitions, and public hearings on zoning and development matters. Significant actions included approving sewer revenue bonds for wastewater system improvements, authorizing zoning changes for battery energy storage systems, and holding public hearings on multiple rezoning requests involving residential developments ranging from 14 to 120 units across the county. One supervisor—Patrick S. Herrity—dissented on a report regarding General Assembly activities, the only vote that deviated from unanimity.
notable: Sewer revenue bonds approved to finance wastewater system improvements
Battery energy storage zoning changes advanced through public hearing
Supervisor Herrity cast sole 'no' vote on General Assembly activities report

fairfax-bos-2026-03-03.digest: The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors passed 25 items on March 3, 2026, all unanimous except for two housing-related votes. Supervisor Patrick Herrity opposed $2 million in financing for Coralain Gardens apartment renovations and abstained from designating that property with a housing designation. The board approved $8.1 million in loans for Agape Chantilly House development, expedited processing for two rezoning applications, and $2 million in subordinate financing for housing renovations, while also endorsing a Route 29 shared-use paths project and approving routine programs for forest health, mosquito control, and Head Start services.
notable: Supervisor Herrity opposed $2M Coralain Gardens financing and abstained on its housing designation; board approved 9-1 and 9-1 with abstention respectively.
Board approved $8.1M in loans for Agape Chantilly House mixed-use development in Chantilly.
Board endorsed design for Route 29 southbound shared-use paths project in Sully District and supported Virginia Passenger Rail Authority's third-track project on Occoquan River corridor.

fairfax-bos-2026-02-17.digest: The Board of Supervisors unanimously approved 23 items including three residential rezonings (one allowing 120 multifamily units in Franconia, another enabling 36 dwelling units per acre in residential development), expedited processing for Nova Petroleum Realty development in Mount Vernon, and expansion of the Reston Community Parking District. The board also approved fee waivers for the Lions Club flea market and First Baptist Church, adopted a strategic communications plan for housing, and authorized solar panel installation at county facilities. One abstention was noted from Supervisor Patrick S. Herrity on an unnamed administrative matter.
notable: Three residential rezonings approved unanimously, including 120 multifamily units for Franconia Development Partners and expedited processing for Nova Petroleum Realty in Mount Vernon District.
All 23 items passed with unanimous or near-unanimous votes; one supervisor (Patrick S. Herrity) abstained on an administrative matter.
Reston Community Parking District boundaries expanded and county facilities authorized for solar panel leasing.

fairfax-bos-2026-02-03.digest: The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors unanimously passed 26 items, including amendments to the 2025 Comprehensive Plan, endorsement of Governor Spanberger's transportation infrastructure study, support for noise mitigation at Reagan National Airport, and designation of 2026 as the 'Year of the New Space Economy.' The board also approved expedited processing for Peterson Companies development applications and held public hearings on rezoning and permit modifications for commercial and religious properties. All votes were 9-0 with no dissent recorded.
notable: Comprehensive Plan amended and site-specific plan application reprioritized with unanimous support
Board designated 2026 'Year of the New Space Economy' and backed airport noise mitigation efforts
No failed motions or dissenting votes recorded; all items passed unanimously

fairfax-bos-2026-01-13.digest: The Board of Supervisors held a routine January organizational meeting with unanimous votes on 31 items, mostly ceremonial recognitions and administrative approvals. Key actions included approving expedited rezoning for High Ridge, setting the 2026 meeting schedule, approving road abandonments and additions in Braddock District, and holding public hearings on seven land-use matters (vehicle repair services, school expansion, church signage modifications, adult day care, residential mixed-use development, alley vacations, and parking permit expansion), all passing without recorded opposition. No substantive policy changes or budget matters were decided.
notable: Expedited rezoning approval granted for High Ridge development (RZ-2025-SP-00003).
Seven land-use public hearings held and approved unanimously, including school expansion, mixed-use residential development, and permit parking expansion.
All 31 items passed unanimously with no dissenting votes recorded.

fairfax-bos-2025-11-18.digest: The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors unanimously passed 31 resolutions on November 18, 2025, mostly consisting of ceremonial recognitions, zoning reviews, and development approvals. The only dissenting vote came from Supervisor Patrick S. Herrity, who opposed a $15 million authorization for the Redevelopment and Housing Authority to acquire property in Providence District for housing development. The board also approved several land-use changes including a mixed-use Tysons rezoning, Lighthouse Baptist Church construction in Franconia District with fee waiver, and modifications to Oakcrest School's grade configuration and various development plans across the county.
notable: Patrick S. Herrity voted no on $15 million housing property acquisition in Providence District; all other votes were 9-0.
Board approved major Tysons mixed-use rezoning with 9.01 floor area ratio and held public hearings on multiple development projects including Rock Hill Road and Fair Oaks Business Park.
Board denied settlement demand in McCaffery v. Fairfax County lawsuit (voted twice on this matter).

fairfax-bos-2025-10-28.digest: The Fairfax County Board of Supervisors held a meeting dominated by unanimous procedural and ceremonial actions, with no dissenting votes recorded. Substantive decisions included authorizing right-of-way acquisition for the Mount Vernon Gateway Phase II project, confirming Leslie B. Johnson as Zoning Administrator, approving a comprehensive plan amendment for Merrifield in Providence District, and conducting public hearings on multiple zoning matters including rezoning for Union Investment and Boston Properties developments, a special exception for New Life Christian Church's outdoor recreation facility, and the Richmond Highway Bus Rapid Transit project's land acquisition. The board also approved donating county vehicles and held hearings on permit parking expansion and street vacation in the Edison area.
notable: Right-of-way acquisition approved for Mount Vernon Gateway Phase II project and comprehensive plan amendment authorized for Merrifield Land Sub-Unit G2.
Leslie B. Johnson confirmed as Fairfax County Zoning Administrator with unanimous 9-0 vote.

Public hearings conducted on major developments including Union Investment office expansion, New Life Christian Church special exception for outdoor recreation with increased height, and Richmond Highway Bus Rapid Transit land acquisition.

princewilliam-bos-2026-06-02.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved a $1.2 million annual traffic enforcement program with four new positions (opposed by Supervisor Stewart) and moved forward amendments to zoning and administrative procedures to comply with 13 state housing and development bills. The board also authorized the Stepping Up Initiative to reduce incarcerated individuals with mental illness through community services (opposed by Supervisor Vega) and approved a satellite voting office expansion for future elections. Two supervisors were absent and Supervisor Vega opposed both the Pride Month proclamation and the detention center initiative.
notable: $1.2 million annual budget for automated traffic enforcement program passed 5-1, with Supervisor Stewart dissenting.
Supervisor Vega opposed both the LGBTQIA+ Pride Month proclamation and the Stepping Up Initiative to reduce mentally ill detainees.
Major zoning amendments approved to comply with 13 state legislative bills affecting housing and development standards.

princewilliam-bos-2026-05-19.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved a household energy rebate fund resolution in a 5-2 vote, with Supervisors Jefferson and Gordy opposing. The board also certified closed meeting minutes and accepted audit reports. Three supervisors were absent from most votes.
notable: Household energy rebate fund approved 5-2, opposed by Jefferson and Gordy
Three supervisors absent during votes (Angry, Bailey, Vega)

princewilliam-bos-2026-05-12.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved 20 items including comprehensive plan amendments for mixed-use development in Catharpin Valley Estates and the Kline Property in Coles District, transportation planning updates for three corridors, and a property settlement at 17870 Fraley Boulevard. Supervisor Angry dissented on two items: the Catharpin Valley Estates rezoning from agriculture to mixed-use and the Sudley Road/Route 234 mobility chapter update. The board sent the Dumfries Flagship Car Wash special use permit and the NOVEC Diamond Hill Substation petition back for further review.
notable: Supervisor Angry voted no on Catharpin Valley Estates mixed-use rezoning and Sudley Road/Route 234 transportation plan; all other votes unanimous.
Board initiated major comprehensive plan changes including Woodlawn area amendment, Kline Property rezoning for development, and three mobility chapter updates.
Dumfries Car Wash permit and NOVEC substation petition remanded for further staff/planning review.

princewilliam-bos-2026-04-21.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved the fiscal year 2027 budget for $1.14 billion including school funding, stormwater and solid waste fees, and a new tax on computer equipment for schools, though supervisors Gordy, Vega, and LaCroix consistently opposed the budget package. The board also approved employee compensation adjustments, extended hiring incentives for hard-to-fill positions, and directed reallocation of Circuit Court Clerk office space for juror use. A 5-3 split marked most major votes, with the three supervisors voting against the budget, tax increases, and supervisor compensation raises.
notable: Fiscal year 2027 budget of $1.14 billion approved 5-3, with Gordy, Vega, and LaCroix opposing.
New tax established on programmable computer equipment and data center equipment to fund school revenue sharing, opposed by same three supervisors.
Stormwater and solid waste fee increases adopted 7-1, with only Vega dissenting.

princewilliam-bos-2026-04-14.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved the sale of county property at 9349 Hornbaker Road to both Patriot Properties, LLC and Hailes Castle, LLC, and decided not to appeal a Virginia Court of Appeals ruling against the county's Digital Gateway rezoning decision from March 31, 2026. Supervisor Vega was appointed to the Potomac and Rappahannock Transportation Commission, abstaining from her own vote. All actions passed unanimously or near-unanimously with no dissenting votes recorded.
notable: County property at 9349 Hornbaker Road sold to two separate entities (Patriot Properties, LLC and Hailes Castle, LLC)
Board chose not to appeal adverse Virginia Court of Appeals decision on Digital Gateway rezoning

princewilliam-bos-2026-04-07.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved amendments to collective bargaining procedures with significant opposition (3 supervisors voted no: Gordy, Vega, and LaCroix), and appointed Brian Landrum to the Planning Commission despite two supervisors voting against the appointment (Jefferson and Stewart). The board also approved development proffers for a Quartz District rezoning project and took action on opioid settlement funds following a closed session.
notable: Collective bargaining code amendment passed 5-3 with Gordy, Vega, and LaCroix opposing
Brian Landrum appointed to Planning Commission with 2 no votes (Jefferson, Stewart) and 1 abstention (Boddye)
Board acted on opioid settlement funds allocation after closed meeting

princewilliam-bos-2026-03-31.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved two major rezoning projects in the Brentsville district—Greenhaven at Broad Run and Village at Broad Run—both passing 7-1 with Supervisor Stewart dissenting. The board appointed a new Deputy County Executive, sent a letter opposing a state budget amendment affecting data center equipment taxation, and held closed sessions on personnel and legal matters. A motion regarding an employee survey related to collective bargaining failed 4-4, with Supervisors Jefferson, Bailey, Boddye, and Stewart opposing it.
notable: Two rezoning approvals for Broad Run developments in Brentsville (Stewart opposed both)
Failed motion on employee survey and collective bargaining—4-4 split vote blocked the directive
Board appointed new Deputy County Executive and opposed state budget amendment on data center taxation

princewilliam-bos-2026-03-17.digest: The Board of County Supervisors transferred $452,473 from county reserves to Prince William County Schools' operating budget in a split 5–3 vote, with Supervisors Gordy, Vega, and LaCroix voting against the measure. The board also approved a lease agreement for county office space in Woodbridge and ended a local emergency declared in January. Most routine items passed unanimously, and three closed-session meetings addressed confidential personnel, legal, and real estate matters.
notable: $452,473 transferred to schools budget: Gordy, Vega, and LaCroix opposed
Board leased new office space at 12701 Marblestone Drive in Woodbridge
Ended local emergency from January 24, 2026

princewilliam-bos-2026-03-10.digest: The Board of County Supervisors appointed Benjamin Baldwin to the Human Rights Commission and modified meeting procedures to add a last-minute agenda item, both over Supervisor Vega's opposition. Supervisor Gordy was absent from the work session.
notable: Benjamin Baldwin appointed to Human Rights Commission
Supervisor Vega opposed waiving standard rules and adding the agenda item
Supervisor Gordy absent

princewilliam-bos-2026-03-03.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved a $361,452 budget adjustment for Community Services, rezoned Prologis 66 Logistical Park from mixed-use to industrial, and approved a fuel station permit in Coles District. However, the board split 4-3 on a rezoning and special use permit for Hornbaker Road in Brentsville District, with supervisors Jefferson, Stewart, and Vega opposing both items. Supervisor Jefferson also abstained on two unspecified motions.
notable: $361,452 allocated to Community Services budget (ongoing and one-time)
Hornbaker Road rezoning and special use permit failed to achieve consensus: 4-3 vote with Jefferson, Stewart, and Vega opposing
Prologis 66 Logistical Park rezoned to industrial use unanimously approved

princewilliam-bos-2026-02-17.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved a violence prevention grant worth $250,000 from the Virginia Department of Criminal Justice Services and authorized two related staff positions. They also greenlit grant applications for infrastructure development in Coles and Occoquan districts, and initiated a Comprehensive Plan Amendment for Fettler Park in the Potomac district. Secretary Candi Mundon King received a commendation, though Supervisor Vega voted against it while Supervisor Gordy abstained; Supervisor Angry was absent throughout the meeting.
notable: Accepted $250,000 Ceasefire Grant for violence prevention program and created two funded positions
Vega opposed commendation for Secretary King (4 aye, 1 no, 1 abstain); Gordy abstained
Initiated Comprehensive Plan Amendment for Fettler Park development

princewilliam-bos-2026-02-10.digest: The Board of County Supervisors held a brief work session and authorized a closed-session meeting with a 5-2 vote, with supervisors Angry and Boddye dissenting. The board then certified the closed meeting was properly conducted and adjourned.
notable: Closed-session motion passed 5-2; supervisors Angry and Boddye opposed

princewilliam-bos-2026-02-03.digest: The Board of County Supervisors ratified a winter weather emergency declaration and approved new affordable housing fund policies in a routine meeting. The board also advanced a comprehensive plan amendment for the La Benison project in Potomac Magisterial District despite opposition from two supervisors, and ratified a tentative police labor agreement with one dissenting vote. All other votes passed unanimously or with minimal abstention.
notable: La Benison comprehensive plan amendment passed 5-2, with Gordy and Vega opposed
Police Association bargaining agreement ratified 6-1, with Vega dissenting
Affordable housing fund policies adopted unanimously

princewilliam-bos-2026-01-20.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved routine business on January 20, including accepting $124,131 in state grant funding for drug testing programs in the Criminal Justice Services department. The board held public hearings on land use matters and conducted a closed session. All votes passed unanimously with Supervisor Franklin absent.
notable: Secured $124,131 in Virginia Supreme Court grant funding for criminal justice drug testing programs

princewilliam-bos-2026-01-13.digest: The Board of County Supervisors held its annual organizational meeting on January 13, 2026, approving routine items including meeting minutes, the 2026 meeting schedule, and electing new Vice Chair and Chair Pro-Tem positions. The board held three closed sessions and took action on matters discussed therein. A motion to reconsider a December 16, 2025 decision on outside legal services for the County Attorney's Office failed 4–4, with Supervisors Angry, Bailey, Boddye, and Franklin opposing reconsideration.
notable: Failed 4–4 motion to reconsider outside legal services decision for County Attorney's Office; Supervisors Angry, Bailey, Boddye, and Franklin voted no on reconsideration
Board held three closed sessions and took action on matters discussed in each

princewilliam-bos-2025-12-16.digest: The Board of County Supervisors allocated $400,000 in legal services funding (opposed by Gordy, Jefferson, and Vega), adopted a Local Foundation and Soil Management Fund ordinance with $200,000 in transferred contingency funds to support foundation and soil management projects countywide, and approved several land-use changes including rezoning in the Occoquan Magisterial District. A directive on the Children's Cabinet failed to pass with a 3-3 split vote (Angry, Bailey, and Vega opposed; Gordy abstained), and the board dissolved the Prince William County Arts Council over objections from Gordy and Jefferson.
notable: $400,000 allocated for County Attorney legal services with 3 supervisors opposed (Gordy, Jefferson, Vega)
Children's Cabinet directive failed: 3-3 vote with Gordy abstaining
Local Foundation and Soil Management Fund ordinance adopted after initial postponement attempt failed

princewilliam-bos-2025-12-09.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved the County Attorney's report and certified closed meeting records. Two supervisors (Stewart and Vega) opposed public hearings on standard agenda items, though the hearings proceeded with a 6-2 vote. The meeting concluded without major action items beyond routine business.
notable: Stewart and Vega voted against holding public hearings on standard agenda items (6-2 passage)

princewilliam-bos-2025-12-02.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved an affordable housing fund policy and initiated a comprehensive plan amendment for the Stadler Place development in Brentsville, though Supervisor Stewart opposed the development plan. Two supervisors were absent. Routine business including meeting minutes and consent agenda items also passed.
notable: Stadler Place development plan amendment passed 5–1, with Stewart dissenting
Affordable Housing Fund policies approved
Two supervisors absent (Bailey, Vega)

princewilliam-bos-2025-11-18.digest: The Board of County Supervisors approved 19 items including a new countywide Local Foundation and Soil Management Fund, two rezoning petitions, and a special use permit, though with some dissent. Supervisor Vega opposed the soil fund establishment and Strathmore rezoning; Supervisors Bailey and Franklin opposed the special use permit for C4A, LLC; and Supervisors Vega and Stewart opposed the Strathmore rezoning petition. The board also authorized $85,000 to hire consultants for a Historic Overlay District Design Guide and acknowledged support for Manassas Park's comprehensive plan amendment.
notable: Board established a countywide Local Foundation and Soil Management Fund with Supervisor Vega's sole dissent.
Special use permit for C4A, LLC in Gainesville district failed to achieve unanimous support (Bailey and Franklin opposed; Jefferson abstained).
Strathmore property rezoning passed 6–2 with Vega and Stewart in opposition.

princewilliam-bos-2025-10-28.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved spending $300,000 on consultant services for labor agreements and allocated $1 million for food insecurity relief if federal nutrition assistance lapses on November 1st; supervisors also hired three new senior officials (Finance Director, Deputy Chief Experience Officer, and Public Service Center Director) and converted a part-time Social Services position to full-time. The board voted to dismiss multiple rezoning and permit applications across five districts and updated noise and traffic ordinances, though Supervisors Vega and Jefferson opposed the noise ordinance changes. One procedural motion to postpone Item 7-J failed 5-2.
notable: $1 million allocated for emergency food relief if federal nutrition assistance stops November 1st
Consultant services funded with $300,000 from contingency for labor agreement work; Supervisors Gordy and Vega opposed
Supervisor Vega consistently voted 'no' or abstained on multiple items (noise ordinance, financing, and procurement matters)

stafford-bos-2026-05-19.digest: The board approved a major Buc-ee's travel center development at a contested location, rezoning the property and vacating right-of-way despite a failed motion to deny it over traffic and comprehensive plan concerns; two supervisors (Guy and Yeung) opposed all three Buc-ee's items. The board also approved a competing Market at Austin Ridge project with fuel sales and storage in a 4–3 split, and green-lit a data center substation and Patawomeck Tribe lease renewal. The meeting extended past midnight to complete discussion.
notable: Buc-ee's rezoning passed 5–2 after a motion to deny failed 5–2, with Crystal Vanuch, Allen, Diggs, English, and Evans voting no on denial but the majority reversing course to approve.
Guy and Pamela Yeung opposed all three Buc-ee's items (rezoning, CUP, right-of-way vacation); Crystal Vanuch opposed rezoning and parking restrictions but backed the data center substation.
Two competing commercial projects approved: Buc-ee's travel center and Market at Austin Ridge with conflicting fuel and storage uses.

stafford-bos-2026-05-05.digest: The Board of Supervisors unanimously approved eleven measures, including $375,000 in prior-year funds for Hidden Lake Service District road repairs damaged by winter weather, purchase of development rights for county land conservation projects, and a new PDR Administrator position. The board also extended timelines for two major development projects (GDC and Enon Road Tech Park) due to planning requirements, approved a six-year county lease with an early-exit option, and authorized engineering services for water treatment facility upgrades. All votes were 7-0 with no dissent or failed motions.
notable: Winter road damage repair approved: $375,000 in prior-year funds allocated to Hidden Lake Service District.
Development project timelines extended due to planning and ordinance amendment delays at GDC and Enon Road Tech Park.
New Purchase of Development Rights Administrator position created; Jennifer Himmelstein appointed to the role.

stafford-bos-2026-04-28.digest: The Board set the real estate tax rate at $0.9675 per $100 of assessed value after initially failing to pass a lower $0.92 rate, with three supervisors (Crystal Vanuch, English, and Pamela Yeung) consistently voting against the higher rate and the fiscal year 2027 budget. The Board also approved reclaimed water system ordinances allowing data centers and other users to access county water, adjusted related pro-rata fees, authorized a school bond issuance, and approved the fire levy despite one dissenting vote. Multiple votes split 4-3 or 5-2, reflecting significant board disagreement on the budget and tax package.
notable: Real estate tax rate set at $0.9675/$100 after $0.92 proposal failed 2-5 (Vanuch, English, Allen, Evans, Yeung opposed).
FY2027 budget adopted 4-3 with Vanuch, English, and Yeung dissenting.
Reclaimed water systems approved for data centers and other state-approved users with new fee schedules.

stafford-bos-2026-04-21.digest: The board approved $1.5 million in spending (Resolution R26-197), maintained the vehicle personal property tax rate at $5.72, and rezoned two parcels in tax map 44-101A from light industrial to urban commercial to enable a freestanding emergency room. The board also approved an AI policy for county government, granted a data center project time extension (with Supervisor English dissenting), and approved an easement for $610 despite public concern about low compensation. Supervisor Allen was absent throughout; one vote on a residential permit failed to achieve consensus (Supervisors Diggs and Guy opposed, Yeung abstained).
notable: Board approved $1.5M spending resolution and new AI guardrails policy for county operations.
Vehicle personal property tax held at $5.72 with split vote (3 no: Yeung, Evans, Guy); separate approval vote had 2 no (Vanuch, English).
Data center extension passed 5–1 with Supervisor English opposing; emergency room rezoning passed unanimously (7–0).

stafford-bos-2026-04-16.digest: The Board of Supervisors held a budget work session on April 16, 2026, authorizing a closed meeting to discuss legal advice on a proposed electric transmission line project and to evaluate a board appointee's performance. The board also certified a previous closed session regarding the disposition of county-owned real property where public discussion would harm the county's negotiating position.
notable: Board will pursue legal consultation on an electric transmission line project
Supervisor Allen was absent from the vote

stafford-bos-2026-04-07.digest: The Board of Supervisors held a special meeting on April 7, 2026, where they allowed board member Maya Guy to participate remotely and then held a closed-door legal session to discuss an electric transmission line project and performance reviews of board appointees. The board certified that the closed meeting followed Virginia's public-records law and addressed only the stated matters. All substantive votes passed with either 7 ayes or 6 ayes (when Guy was absent).

stafford-bos-2026-03-24.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved routine items including the meeting agenda and closed-session procedures, all unanimously. The board passed Resolution R26-140 opposing state collective bargaining legislation (HB 1263 and SB 378), citing unfunded costs to the county, with Supervisor Pamela Yeung dissenting and Supervisor Evans absent. The board also authorized a closed session to discuss county property disposition where public discussion would undermine negotiating leverage.
notable: Resolution R26-140 opposing state collective bargaining bills passed 5–1, with Supervisor Yeung dissenting over unfunded mandate concerns.
Board authorized closed session on county real property disposition to protect negotiating position.

stafford-bos-2026-03-03.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved the FY2027 tax rate at $0.985 per $100 of assessed value (fire levy at $0.014) after rejecting an alternative proposal with a lower base rate, with English and Crystal Vanuch voting no. The board also approved reclaimed water system rates and policies for data center users and authorized public hearings on reclaimed water service ordinances. Resolution R26-47 on data center construction was tabled pending further review of tax rate implications and timelines.
notable: Tax rate set at $0.985 with fire levy $0.014; English and Crystal Vanuch opposed the measure
Alternative proposal to set rate at $0.93 with data center rate at $2.50 failed 5-2, with Allen, Deuntay Diggs, Evans, Guy, and Pamela Yeung voting no
Resolution R26-47 on data center construction timeline and tax implications tabled for further review

stafford-bos-2026-02-03.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved routine operational matters including Fire Rescue reimbursement resolution, furniture replacement contracts for county facilities ($6–1 vote with Darrell English opposed), and six separate $50,000 dam maintenance grant applications. Two significant development applications failed unanimously or near-unanimously (6–1 votes): one covering a tech park, fast-food restaurant, and medical imaging center, and another for development applications with extensions. The board also cancelled the Onville Road sidewalk project while acknowledging repayment obligations for federal Transportation Alternatives Program funding.
notable: Two development application motions failed 6–1 or worse, blocking a tech park, fast-food restaurant, medical imaging center, and other projects
Onville Road sidewalk project cancelled; county must repay Transportation Alternatives Program funding
Darrell English and Crystal Vanuch split on Stafford-Schools Working Committee timeline adjustment (5–2 vote)

stafford-bos-2026-01-20.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved $18.1 million in strategic spending from the budget reserve, including a new child protective services supervisor and emergency services upgrades, plus funding for a Computer-Aided Dispatch system for fire and rescue. The board also authorized water and sewer revenue bonds (interest capped at 6%), approved leasing county property to a pediatric eye care provider, and adopted zoning changes to permit contractor storage yards in industrial areas. A property use resolution passed 4–3, with Allen, Vanuch, and Yeung opposing; Crystal Vanuch also voted no on a water contractor milestone discussion and a governance review deferral, while a separate zoning review motion on Airbnb regulations failed to gain majority support (5–2, with Diggs and Yeung opposed).
notable: Board approved $18.1M in strategic investments and released full FY2026 budget from the 5% reserve for immediate spending.
Property use resolution passed 4–3 split (Allen, Vanuch, Yeung opposed); Airbnb zoning review motion failed 5–2 (Diggs, Yeung opposed).
Crystal Vanuch dissented on water contractor milestone verification and a deferred governance review.

stafford-bos-2026-01-06.digest: The Board of Supervisors held a chaotic chair election with multiple failed nomination rounds before ultimately deadlocking—Mr. Diggs, Ms. Allen, and Dr. Yeung all failed to secure the chair position, and no vice chair was elected after similar failed votes. Beyond the leadership impasse, the Board unanimously approved routine business including the 2026 meeting calendar, numerous committee and board appointments (with one dissent from Dr. Yeung on the Planning Commission), and rescheduled the October meeting to support National Night Out participation.
notable: Chair election failed entirely after 4 nomination attempts, with no candidate reaching the required threshold.
Dr. Yeung was the sole dissenter on the Planning Commission appointments (6-1 vote); all other major items passed 7-0.
Board approved a closed meeting to discuss an electric transmission line project and board appointee reviews.

stafford-bos-2025-12-16.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved major water infrastructure and financing decisions on December 16, including Ordinance O25-41 for treatment facility supply plans, borrowing up to $3.85% interest from Virginia Resources Authority for a water project, and extended Planning Commission timelines to finalize a Water, Sewer, and Reclaimed Water System Master Plan. Emergency relief was granted by setting personal property tax penalties and interest to 0% until January 9, 2026, and the board declared a Drought Watch authorizing voluntary water restrictions. All votes passed unanimously (7-0) except for a reconsideration of the Planning Commission extension, which had split opposition from Supervisors English and Vanuch (5-2), and a subsequent vote on that reconsideration that failed 6-1 with Supervisor Allen opposing.
notable: Board approved water system master plan timeline extension despite split votes (English and Vanuch opposed the reconsideration; Allen opposed the subsequent vote).
County authorized borrowing at 3.85% interest for water infrastructure project via Virginia Resources Authority.
Drought Watch declared with authority for voluntary water restrictions; personal property tax penalties waived through January 9, 2026.

stafford-bos-2025-12-02.digest: The Board approved spending $350,000 on septic system pumping services for county facilities, with supervisor Darrell English dissenting, and allocated funds for road design work and an emergency services data integration project. The Board also raised the Treasurer's salary by $15,500 retroactive to October and passed a zoning ordinance clarifying development impact standards, though supervisors Meg Bohmke and Crystal Vanuch opposed a proclamation honoring a community member. A motion to reconsider the Consent Agenda passed but with dissent from two supervisors.
notable: Board approved $350,000 septic pumping contract (Darrell English opposed)
Treasurer salary increased $15,500 retroactive to October 8, 2025
Emergency services data system integration project approved unanimously

stafford-bos-2025-11-18.digest: The Board of Supervisors met on November 18 with one member absent and passed five items unanimously among those present: a procedural agenda amendment, authorization for documentation and a potential public hearing on a lease or arrangement, a resolution to advertise a public hearing on updated development ordinances per state requirements, an ordinance updating business regulations including cryptocurrency industry oversight, and a resolution on bridge naming and easement matters despite incomplete public exhibits. All votes were 6-1 in favor (with Crystal Vanuch absent).
notable: All five measures passed unanimously among the six supervisors present; Ordinance O25-26 addressed cryptocurrency industry oversight and consumer protection from mislabeled items.
Resolution R25-249 on bridge naming and easements was approved despite incomplete public exhibits and missing trail/sidewalk location information.

stafford-bos-2025-11-06.digest: The Board of Supervisors approved 15 measures, including a $65,000 half-day holiday pay bonus for county employees (opposed by Crystal Vanuch and Pamela Yeung), $45,000 annual funding for Gari Melcher's visitor center gift shop, and a resolution to purchase rather than rent modular office trailers for the Utilities Department. The board also advanced a master plan for reclaimed water systems to serve data centers, scheduled a December 2 public hearing on zoning ordinance changes with grandfathering for pre-October data centers, and approved a settlement agreement for the Stafford Technology Campus development phases.
notable: County staff will receive an extra half-day holiday pay on November 26 costing $65,000, with supervisors Vanuch and Yeung voting no.
Board scheduled December 2 public hearing to reordain zoning ordinance with grandfathering provisions for data center projects approved before October.
Approved $45,000 annual funding for Gari Melcher's visitor center gift shop and a golf tournament event expected to generate $2 million in economic impact.

newyork-bos-2026-06-30.digest: The City Council passed 25 items in a stated meeting on June 30, 2026, including Fiscal Year 2027 budget resolutions (Resolutions 540-546), appropriations and capital budget modifications, and a Community Development Program. Most votes passed with large majorities, though a consistent bloc of five councilmembers (Ariola, Carr, Morano, Paladino, Vernikov) opposed many resolutions, while some legislation saw broader dissent from progressive members on public safety and small business issues. All items passed despite the opposition.
notable: Six councilmembers (Ariola, Carr, Morano, Paladino, Vernikov, and either Felder or Stevens depending on item) regularly voted no on FY2027 budget and spending resolutions.
Introduction 929-A (public safety) passed 39-10 with 2 abstentions, with ten progressive members (Avilés, Cabán, De La Rosa, Gutiérrez, Hanif, Nurse, and others) voting no.
Introduction 553-A (small business) passed 44-4 with 3 abstentions, receiving opposition from both Avilés, Cabán, and conservative bloc members.

newyork-bos-2026-06-30-Committee-on-Fina.digest: The Finance Committee approved the city's Fiscal Year 2027 budget and related resolutions on June 30, 2026, including appropriation changes, expense schedules, and a community development program. Committee member Morano opposed most items, and Wong joined Morano in opposing Resolutions 530–532 and 534–535; Gennaro and Marte were absent. All 21 votes passed despite the dissent.
notable: Fiscal Year 2027 budget and appropriations approved with Morano voting no on nearly every item and Wong joining him on several resolutions
Committee members Gennaro and Marte absent from the entire meeting

newyork-bos-2026-06-30-Committee-on-Publ.digest: The Committee on Public Safety passed Introductory Number 929-A, a public safety bill, by a vote of 7-2, with committee members Gutiérrez and Nurse voting against it.
notable: Bill 929-A passed 7-2; Gutiérrez and Nurse opposed

newyork-bos-2026-06-30-Committee-on-Fina.digest: The Finance Committee approved changes to city funding allocations for various organizations and set property tax interest rates for fiscal year 2027, with rates varying by property value: 6% for properties under $250,000, 9% for properties between $250,000 and $450,000, and 2.5% for owners on installment payment plans. Councilmembers Morano and Wong voted against the higher-value property tax rate, while two committee members were absent throughout the meeting.
notable: Property tax interest rates for 2027 established with tiered structure based on property value
Morano and Wong opposed the 9% rate on properties valued $250k-$450k
Councilmembers Gennaro and Marte absent for entire meeting

newyork-bos-2026-06-25.digest: The Committee on Land Use approved a zoning application for Dewitt Clinton Park North at 801 Eleventh Avenue in Manhattan. Both votes passed without dissent, with one member absent.

newyork-bos-2026-06-11.digest: The City Council passed two resolutions: one requiring the Mayor's Office of Immigrant Affairs to create clear signage about city property access rules (passed 44-6 with six members opposing), and another approving Commissioner of Deeds appointments or actions (passed 50-0). Council Member Farías was absent from both votes.
notable: Signage resolution opposed by Ariola, Carr, Morano, Paladino, Vernikov, and Wong (6 no votes)
Commissioner of Deeds measure passed unanimously with one absent

newyork-bos-2026-06-11-Committee-on-Fina.digest: The Finance Committee approved a budget modification under the City Charter and a land use application for St. John's Flats in Brooklyn. Both measures passed with 15 votes in favor; Gennaro and Marte were absent.

la-primegov-17619.digest: The City Council unanimously advanced alcohol beverage licenses for three locations (8th & Fig Project, US Fuels Service Station, and Costco), heard protests on seven street lighting districts, and passed new fire-safe landscaping rules aligned with state wildfire prevention mandates.
