The council also approved bridge housing funding, accepted several police equipment donations (with four council members opposing two of the police-related donations), repealed a health insurance option, and removed nine properties from the Rent Escrow Account Program. Council members Blumenfield and Hernandez were frequently absent; no motions failed.
notable: Four council members (Hernandez, Jurado, Raman, Soto-Martínez) opposed accepting LAPD equipment donations, with another split 10-3 vote opposing a separate police technology donation.
City established new fire-safe landscaping requirements for residential properties to reduce wildfire risk under Governor's Executive Order N-18-25.
Council approved continued three-year lease for bridge housing/low-barrier navigation center supporting homelessness initiatives.

la-primegov-17637.digest: The City Council passed 46 items unanimously (12 aye, 3 absent) in a largely administrative meeting dominated by code violation lien hearings and legal strategy discussions. The council approved emergency declarations for January 2025 windstorms and wildfires, explored a video production grant program, requested a spending plan for bus lane violation enforcement revenue, and approved a Caltrans grant application for the Ballona Creek Bike Path. The vast majority of the agenda—roughly 30 items—consisted of hearings on property owner protests against Building and Safety Department liens for code and nuisance abatement costs at multiple addresses throughout the city, plus two street/walkway closures. Three councilmembers (Blumenfield, Padilla, and Yaroslavsky) were absent from all votes.
notable: Council approved emergency declarations stemming from January 2025 windstorms and wildfires in Los Angeles.
Nearly 30 code violation lien hearings were held for properties city-wide, with all protests heard but outcomes not specified in the record.
Council explored a new grant program to fund local video content creators and requested a spending plan for bus lane violation enforcement revenue.

la-primegov-17683.digest: The City Council passed 14 items unanimously (or near-unanimously with three councils members absent from each vote), including a five-year infrastructure and technology plan, conversion of streetlights to solar-to-battery systems, establishment of oversize vehicle parking restrictions, and appointment of Daniel Tabor to the Police Commission. The parking restrictions motion drew three dissenting votes from Hernandez, Jurado, and Soto-Martínez, though it passed 9–3. Other actions designated a Japanese American Community Center Square intersection, closed an alley near the Wilmington Municipal Building, and approved funding for downtown San Pedro pedestrian improvements.
notable: Streetlight conversion to solar-to-battery power authorized, with vacated electrical conduits to be repurposed or leased.
Oversize vehicle parking restrictions passed 9–3 with Hernandez, Jurado, and Soto-Martínez opposing.
Five-year Capital and Technology Improvement Plan for municipal infrastructure approved.

la-primegov-17614.digest: City Council passed 35 items including 11 street lighting district maintenance ordinances (all unanimous among present members), confirmed liens on five properties for code violations and nuisance abatement costs, allocated funding for various district projects and cultural events, designated Crenshaw and 54th Street as James "Jim" McBeth Square, and accepted three armored vehicles for LAPD. Three council members (Blumenfield, Lee, Price Jr.) were absent from most votes; the armored vehicle donation passed 9-0 with six members absent and drew less support than other measures.
notable: Council approved 11 separate street lighting district maintenance ordinances; all passed with identical 12-0 votes.
Armored vehicle donation for LAPD passed 9-0 with six members absent—the lowest support of any item voted on.
Council requested City Attorney draft housing regulation updates (repeated three times in the record).

la-primegov-17618.digest: The City Council approved 20 items with near-unanimous votes, including funding for Animal Services pet fairs, converting recreation facilities into art galleries in Council District 14, and a third amendment to the North Haiwee Dam seismic project contract. A recycling program component was eliminated over dissent from McOsker, Padilla, and Raman; LAPD drone funding passed despite opposition from Hernandez and Soto-Martínez; and a motion to explore police hiring funding split 9–4 against, with Hernandez, Jurado, Raman, and Soto-Martínez opposed. Five council members were absent from most votes.
notable: Removing Barriers to Recycling program ended despite three no votes (McOsker, Padilla, Raman)
LAPD drone funding approved 11–2 (Hernandez and Soto-Martínez opposed)
Police hiring exploration failed 9–4 in final vote (Hernandez, Jurado, Raman, Soto-Martínez opposed)

la-primegov-17731.digest: The City Council passed multiple measures including parking restrictions amendments in Harbor Gateway (opposed by Hernandez, Jurado, and Soto-Martínez), enforcement designations for sitting/lying ordinances in Council District 5, Charter Reform Commission disclosure requirements for ex parte communications, and examined numerous ballot measures and revenue options for 2026 elections. Most actions passed with near-unanimous support, though one revenue examination motion failed decisively (2 aye, 12 no) with broad opposition. Councilmember Price Jr. was absent for all votes.
notable: Parking restrictions amendment for Harbor Gateway passed 11-3, with Hernandez, Jurado, and Soto-Martínez opposing.
One revenue/ballot measure examination motion failed 2-12 with widespread opposition including Blumenfield, Harris-Dawson, Hernandez, Hutt, Jurado, and Lee voting no.
Council approved Charter Reform Commission disclosure requirements for ex parte communications with near-unanimous support.

la-primegov-17737.digest: Los Angeles City Council passed 38 items on January 28, 2026, with near-unanimous support. Major decisions included: rezoning properties in West Los Angeles neighborhoods to enable new housing, streamlining sign and tenant improvement reviews in the Ventura-Cahuenga corridor, establishing a grant program for local video creators, proposing a cashless retail transaction ban, and increasing funding for the Time-Limited Subsidy Program for homeless services. Two motions drew dissent: accepting Metro funding for the Open and Slow Streets Program (Blumenfield and Rodriguez opposed), and increasing legal fees for the LA Alliance litigation (Lee, McOsker, Padilla, and Rodriguez opposed). Council Member Price Jr. was absent from all items; Nazarian was absent from five. The remainder consisted of routine financial approvals, property removals from rent programs, and closed-session litigation discussions.
notable: Rezoning in West Los Angeles to enable new housing development under the CD 5 Housing Element
Split vote on Metro funding acceptance: Blumenfield and Rodriguez voted no; split vote on LA Alliance litigation funding: four council members (Lee, McOsker, Padilla, Rodriguez) opposed
Ban on cashless retail transactions proposed; grant program for video content creators approved

la-primegov-17717.digest: The City Council approved $40 million in bonds for a 128-unit affordable housing development and $85 million for an 11-story hotel, along with new salary schedules for city jobs, a firefighters union contract amendment, and various departmental and land-use matters. All items passed with overwhelming support (12–14 votes in favor), with three council members absent throughout the meeting. Notable items included launching a fentanyl detection device pilot program and approving animal welfare services at a city facility.
notable: $125 million in bonded debt authorized for affordable housing and hotel development
Fentanyl detection device pilot program launched using opioid settlement funds
No dissenting votes recorded; three council members (Lee, Nazarian, Price Jr.) absent for most items

la-primegov-17711.digest: The city council approved housing and budget reports for fiscal year 2024-25, including updates on the state shelter crisis declaration and rideshare trust fund. The board also approved multiple amendments to the Department of Water and Power's software maintenance contract with OGMA Consulting Corporation, and removed a property at 3034 East Chaucer Street from the Rent Escrow Account Program. All measures passed unanimously among council members present, though five members were absent.
notable: Multiple amendments to DWP software maintenance contract passed without dissent
Property at 3034 East Chaucer Street removed from Rent Escrow Account Program
Five council members absent for most votes during the meeting

la-primegov-17180.digest: The City Council passed dozens of routine items including street lighting district ordinances, property leases and sales, and parking restrictions in multiple districts. Three council members (Nazarian, Price Jr., and Raman) were absent for most votes. The council approved a revised Charter Communications contract, authorized acquiring property to expand the electric bus depot, and created multiple ballot measures for cannabis business taxes and transient occupancy taxes—though one transient occupancy tax measure failed 7–8, with Jurado, Hutt, Lee, McOsker, Nazarian, and Park opposing. Notably, the council voted 15–0 to prohibit city funding for the Nowruz event and directed funding for an LGBTQ crosswalk on Abbot Kinney.
notable: One transient occupancy tax ballot measure failed 7–8 with six council members voting no (Jurado, Hutt, Lee, McOsker, Nazarian, Park); three other related tax ballot measures passed with either unanimous or near-unanimous support.
Three council members (Hernandez, Jurado, Soto-Martínez) dissented on all three oversized vehicle parking restrictions, which otherwise passed 12–3.
The council voted 15–0 to prohibit city funding for the Nowruz event and separately voted 15–0 to direct funding for an LGBTQ crosswalk on Abbot Kinney.

la-primegov-17188.digest: The City Council passed 52 items including authorization of $1.9 million in tax-exempt bonds for Voltaire Villas Supportive Housing, approval of a staffing plan to reduce outside legal spending, acceptance of easements for street and traffic signal improvements, and allocation of Measure A 2025 grant funding for recreation and parks projects. One item drew dissent: three council members (Hernandez, Jurado, Soto-Martínez) voted no on accepting a state grant for traffic safety enforcement in FY 2025-26, though it passed 9–3. Four council members were consistently absent (Hutt, Nazarian, Rodriguez, Yaroslavsky), with Park absent on five votes. The majority of the agenda comprised routine closed-session litigation discussions with legal counsel.
notable: Council authorized $1.9 million in tax-exempt bonds for Voltaire Villas Supportive Housing Project.
Traffic safety grant acceptance failed to achieve consensus: three council members (Hernandez, Jurado, Soto-Martínez) voted no, though the measure passed 9–3.
City Attorney staffing plan approved to reduce reliance on outside counsel and lower legal spending.

la-primegov-17752.digest: The City Council advanced a continued hearing on a building lien dispute, received the 2028 Olympics organizing committee's annual report and sustainability plan (focused on community business support), and approved an exemption for one Housing Department position from civil service rules. The housing exemption drew three no votes from Nazarian, Padilla, and Rodriguez.
notable: Housing Department position exempted from civil service with 3 council members opposed (Nazarian, Padilla, Rodriguez)
Council received 2028 Olympics impact and sustainability plan with emphasis on community business procurement
Significantly low attendance: 5 council members absent during the meeting

la-primegov-17798.digest: City Council approved $30.6 million in bonds for a 97-unit housing development on North Main Street and passed two tax increases: a new cannabis business tax and higher transient occupancy tax (hotel tax), with Councilmembers Lee and Rodriguez voting no on both tax measures. Council also approved street vacations in District 11, authorized an alcohol beverage license at East Damon Street, removed multiple properties from the Rent Escrow Account Program, and approved numerous district-specific funding items for park improvements, security, and special events. Three councilmembers were absent throughout the meeting.
notable: $30.6M housing bond and two tax increases (cannabis and hotel) passed with 2-vote dissent from Lee and Rodriguez on the taxes
Multiple properties removed from Rent Escrow Account Program and street vacations approved for District 11
Requests for reports on hillside fire zone clearing authority and cannabis enforcement, with 3 councilmembers (Hernandez, Jurado, Soto-Martínez) opposing fire zone changes

la-primegov-17872.digest: The City Council passed 22 items in a single meeting, including authorization of up to $3.2 million in tax-exempt bonds for supportive housing at 803 East 5th Street, approval of grocery store pricing transparency legislation, and amendments to various service agreements at the airport and harbor. Four council members were absent from most votes (Jurado, Lee, Padilla, and Price Jr.), while all items passed unanimously among those present with no dissenting votes or failed motions.
notable: $3.2 million in tax-exempt bonds authorized for 803 East 5th Street Supportive Housing Project
Grocery store pricing transparency ordinance approved requiring physical coupons with matching prices
All 22 items passed with no dissent—no split votes or failed motions

la-primegov-17712.digest: The City Council held 21 hearings on property owner protests against Building and Safety Department liens for code violation costs across multiple addresses, all passing with 11–12 votes in favor. Beyond these routine hearings, the council approved an $85 million qualified nonprofit bond to finance an 11-story hotel at 1130 South Hope Street, made operational the Unarmed Model of Crisis Response (UMCR) program on a permanent basis, authorized the sale of city-owned property at 438 North Mesa Street, and increased Port of Los Angeles tariff rates indexed to consumer prices. Two council members (Nazarian and Rodriguez) opposed exempting Los Angeles Housing Department positions from civil service requirements. Three council members (Hutt, Lee, Price Jr.) were consistently absent.
notable: Council permanently adopted the Unarmed Model of Crisis Response (UMCR) crisis intervention program, directing improvements to dispatch and technology.
Approved $85 million in bonds to finance an 11-story hotel at 1130 South Hope Street downtown.
Two dissenting votes (Nazarian, Rodriguez) against exempting Housing Department positions from civil service requirements.

la-primegov-17718.digest: The City Council held hearings on 20 nuisance abatement liens across the city, all of which passed with minimal opposition. Beyond lien approvals, the council allocated $819,295 in redevelopment bond proceeds for the Liechty School Safety Project, approved operating leases for two homeless services facilities (A Bridge Home shelter and Tiny Home Village), and designated the Sunkist Growers headquarters as a Historic-Cultural Monument. The council also directed staff to study raising the small business tax exemption threshold and study support programs for struggling restaurants. Four council members (Lee, Park, Blumenfield, and Yaroslavsky) were absent for most votes.
notable: Council approved $819,295 in funding for Liechty School Safety Project from redevelopment bond proceeds.
Council authorized two major homeless services facility leases: A Bridge Home shelter and Tiny Home Village housing site.
Sunkist Growers headquarters building designated as a Historic-Cultural Monument with unanimous support among voting members.

la-primegov-17893.digest: The City Council passed 17 items by wide margins, primarily approving property deals and committee reports with five council members absent throughout. Key actions include authorizing the sale of city-owned parcels at North El Centro Avenue and West Afton Place, approving a license agreement for Canoga Park Community Center Foundation at Owensmouth Avenue, and authorizing a vehicle storage lease with Caltrans for LAPD at Ramirez Street. The council also approved a pedestrian mall proposal on Mulholland Highway to improve access to Griffith Park and designated three community squares honoring Francisco Morazan and local leaders Yvonne Chan and Anita Zepeda.
notable: City authorized sale of surplus properties at 1345 North El Centro Avenue and 6207 West Afton Place
Council approved pedestrian mall on Mulholland Highway (blocks 5600-5900) to provide public access to Griffith Park
Five council members absent for most votes (Nazarian, Park, Price Jr., Raman, Rodriguez)

la-primegov-17800.digest: City Council passed 28 measures including three street lighting district improvements, authorization for speed safety cameras and electronic traffic citations, a ban on oversized vehicle parking in District 10, eviction defense and homelessness prevention services (with Hernandez, Jurado, Raman, and Soto-Martínez opposed), mental health facility bonds for Districts 13-14, and a 395-unit rental housing project on Vanowen Street. Councilmembers Park and Soto-Martínez were frequently absent. The most contentious votes were the eviction defense services contract (4 no votes) and oversized vehicle parking bans (2 no votes each from Hernandez and Jurado).
notable: Eviction defense and homelessness prevention contract passed 10-4 with Hernandez, Jurado, Raman, and Soto-Martínez dissenting.
Speed safety cameras and electronic traffic citations both approved unanimously to modernize enforcement.
395-unit rental housing project authorized at 21010 Vanowen Street in Council District 3 via revenue bonds.

la-primegov-17916.digest: The City Council passed 35 items with minimal dissent, though five members were absent. Major actions included directing reports on residential development near LAX, establishing new film industry incentives (streamlined permitting, soundstage certification, free small-scale permits), removing nine properties from the Rent Escrow Account Program, and authorizing street enforcement against homeless encampments in Council District 10—the only contentious vote, with four council members opposing. Three animal services and historic monument appointments also advanced.
notable: Council District 10 homeless enforcement zone passed 9–4 with Hernandez, Jurado, Raman, and Soto-Martínez opposed.
Nine rental properties removed from the Rent Escrow Account Program (which holds tenant payments during landlord violations).
Major film industry push: fast-track permits, soundstage certification program, and free permits for small-scale public filming.

la-primegov-17877.digest: The City Council passed 14 items with unanimous support (11 aye, 4 absent each) including approval of a housing development initiative, multipurpose field conversion at the Obama Sports Complex, vending and playground equipment contracts, and a feasibility study for a new park at Degnan Plaza. Key actions included renaming soccer fields after Dr. George Pla, establishing e-bike regulations at nature preserves, and declaring infeasible a reclaimed water project phase at Griffith Park. Notably, four council members were absent from every vote: Nazarian, Padilla, and Yaroslavsky appeared on all absences, while McOsker missed most votes.
notable: Council approved the Small Lots, Big Impacts housing initiative and advanced feasibility study for a new park at Degnan Plaza
All 14 motions passed with 11 ayes and four consistent absences; no dissent or failed motions recorded
Notable absences: Nazarian, Padilla, and Yaroslavsky absent from every vote

la-primegov-17890.digest: The City Council approved an $85 million bond to finance an 11-story hotel on South Hope Street and established a new zoning plan for San Pedro to allow phased redevelopment. The Council also moved forward with multiple service contracts for eviction defense and homelessness prevention, though Councilmembers Hernandez, Jurado, Hutt, Raman, and Lee registered scattered opposition to different contract iterations. Additionally, the Council approved alcoholic beverage licenses for on-site and off-site consumption at two locations and required the Dodgers to reimburse the city for police and fire resources spent on World Series events.
notable: $85 million hotel bond and new San Pedro zoning plan both passed without dissent
Multiple Council members voted against various homelessness prevention service contracts, with opposition ranging from 1 to 4 votes depending on the iteration
Dodgers required to reimburse city for municipal resources supporting World Series events

la-primegov-17921.digest: The City Council raised emergency services fees for ambulance transport and mileage, increased police commission and business permit fees, and authorized $29.7 million in tax-exempt financing for a supportive housing project. Five council members (Hernandez, Jurado, Nazarian, Raman, Soto-Martínez) opposed a legal services agreement for police litigation defense. The council also passed measures to track corporate ownership of housing stock, establish a pilot program monetizing city assets, and create reporting on the BuildLA development project.
notable: Five council members voted against authorizing legal services for police litigation defense (Hernandez, Jurado, Nazarian, Raman, Soto-Martínez opposed; 10-5 vote).
$29.7 million approved for Prisma Apartments supportive housing project in Council District 13.
Emergency services charges increased for ambulance transport and mileage fees; council also approved tracking of corporate housing consolidation and its effects on renters.

la-primegov-17719.digest: City Council passed 59 items on March 25, 2026, with four members absent. Significant approvals included oversize vehicle parking restrictions in Council Districts 3 and 12 (with three councilmembers dissenting), mid-year budget updates for FY 2025-26 (which drew five dissenting votes on one version), affordable housing and immigration enforcement protections, facility improvements for water and power infrastructure, and acceptance of state grants for social services and cannabis equity. The council also approved updates to planning application fees, sidewalk easements, and removed nine properties from the Rent Escrow Account Program.
notable: Mid-year FY 2025-26 budget report drew split votes, with one version receiving only 8 ayes and 5 noes from Hernandez, Jurado, Nazarian, Raman, and Soto-Martínez opposing.
Oversize vehicle parking restrictions in Districts 3 and 12 passed 11-3 with Hernandez, Jurado, and Soto-Martínez dissenting on both.
Council directed preparation of ordinances on immigration enforcement protections and illegal signage penalties, and approved middle-income affordable housing pilot program.

la-primegov-17751.digest: The City Council held a lengthy meeting reviewing dozens of administrative reports and approving several infrastructure and recovery initiatives. Major actions included establishing a Disaster Recovery District to support the Palisades Fire recovery area with funding for a feasibility study, approving environmental permits and right-of-way changes for the Alameda Street widening project and Canoga Avenue modifications, and adopting a worker transition plan for the Phillips 66 refinery closure. The Council also appointed three members to the Board of Fire Commissioners with one dissenting vote (Councilmember Nazarian opposed John Perez's appointment), accepted departmental reports on transportation revenue programs, disability accessibility compliance, and pension matters, and approved new procedures for micro-trenching underground work with restoration fees. Four council members were absent throughout most of the meeting.
notable: Disaster Recovery District established for Palisades Fire area with feasibility study funding approved unanimously.
Councilmember Nazarian dissented on John Perez's appointment to the Board of Fire Commissioners (13-1 vote).
Alameda Street widening project environmental permits approved, plus Canoga Avenue right-of-way modification to align with Collector Street designation.

la-primegov-17891.digest: The council passed approximately 70 items with near-unanimous support, including three special assessment districts for street lighting improvements, two new paid parking meter zones on West Adams Boulevard, and authorization for up to $110 million in revenue bonds for a 316-unit apartment complex. Notable split votes occurred on a $2.1 million police drone program donation (Hernandez and Jurado opposed), police services funding in Council District 12 (Hernandez and Soto-Martínez opposed), and a police administration building project (Soto-Martínez opposed). The council also held hearings on protests against two business improvement districts, accepted multiple smaller donations for police programs and community initiatives, and allocated funding for homeless services, FIFA World Cup events, and various council district projects.
notable: $110 million revenue bonds approved for 316-unit apartment complex at 5909 Variel Avenue
$2.1 million police drone program donation opposed by Hernandez and Jurado; additional police services in CD-12 also drew opposition from Hernandez and Soto-Martínez
Three new street lighting special assessment districts established and two paid parking meter zones approved on West Adams Boulevard

la-primegov-18052.digest: The City Council approved a sprawling agenda dominated by naming four public squares after community figures, advancing pedestrian and traffic safety infrastructure (sidewalk repairs, bike lanes, street takeovers prevention), and temporarily closing several alleys. A split vote rejected oversize vehicle parking restrictions and a Homeland Security grant, with Hernandez, Jurado, and Soto-Martínez opposing both. The council also advanced a rezoning at 9119 North De Soto Avenue and accepted an initiative petition to repeal the Business Gross Receipts Tax, while removing 12 properties from the Rent Escrow Account Program.
notable: Three council members (Hernandez, Jurado, Soto-Martínez) voted no on oversize vehicle parking restrictions on West 81st Street, West Manchester Avenue, Beloit Avenue, and Venice, and again on accepting a Homeland Security grant.
Council accepted an initiative petition to repeal the city's Business Gross Receipts Tax on virtually all businesses—a major revenue decision requiring subsequent voter approval.
Sidewalk repairs under the Pilot Program were declared an urgent necessity as part of broader pedestrian facility improvements citywide.

la-primegov-18101.digest: City Council passed 14 items with overwhelming support, including repairs to the Panorama City splash pad and construction of a new one at Allegheny Park, improvements to Sun Valley Metrolink access, and authorization for Department of General Services to negotiate leases and agreements including a parking lease at 888 Vermont Avenue and a cannabis cultivation licensing process. Four council members were absent for most votes, and all items passed with either 11 or 12 votes in favor and no dissenting votes recorded.
notable: Panorama City splash pad will reopen and a new splash pad will be built at Allegheny Park
Cannabis cultivation and delivery licensing process approved
All 14 items passed unanimously with no recorded opposition

la-primegov-17920.digest: The City Council approved an $85 million bond for an 11-story hotel at 1130 South Hope Street and confirmed multiple building-code violation liens on properties across the city. The council also allocated funding for pedestrian facility repairs ahead of the 2028 Olympics, accepted state and federal grants, and funded various community programs and beautification projects. One motion to designate an enforcement location in Council District 9 for sit-lie-sleep ordinance violations drew opposition from four council members (Jurado, Hutt, Raman, and Soto-Martínez), and two staff exemptions from civil service saw dissent from Padilla and Rodriguez.
notable: Council approved $85M in bonds for 11-story hotel at 1130 South Hope Street with 10 yes votes and 5 absences.
Motion to designate Council District 9 enforcement location for sit-lie-sleep ordinance received 8 yes, 4 no (opposed by Jurado, Hutt, Raman, Soto-Martínez); passed anyway.
Two Civil Service exemptions for LA Housing Department positions drew opposition—Director position vote 10-2 (Padilla and Rodriguez opposed), and Principal Coordinator position passed 13-0.

la-primegov-17720.digest: Council held a lengthy session primarily processing 26 Building and Safety property lien hearings for nuisance abatement and code violations across multiple addresses, all approved unanimously. Beyond the liens, Council approved contracts for legal services (Nossaman LLP for tenant relocation law, Susman Godfrey LLP for litigation evaluation with one dissenting vote from Nazarian), authorized the Board of Water and Power to purchase three properties downtown, extended the wildfire emergency declaration, and approved various funding allocations and refinancing measures. Four council members were absent (Harris-Dawson, Park, Rodriguez, Soto-Martinez), reducing voting capacity on most items.
notable: One dissent: Councilmember Nazarian voted no on the Susman Godfrey LLP legal services agreement while 11 voted yes.
Council approved 26 separate lien appeal hearings for properties with code violations, all passing 12-0 (with three absent).
Board of Water and Power authorized to purchase three downtown properties at 865 South Figueroa, 930 West 8th Place, and 825 James M. Wood Boulevard.

la-primegov-18149.digest: City Council approved 10 items on April 24, including appointing Nicole Chase to the Recreation and Parks Board, exempting LA Public Library agreements from competitive contracting rules, and granting The Rescue Train a parking space at an animal services center for spay and neuter services. The council also authorized an agreement with Civic Collective to manage Parks Department merchandise sales, extended funding for the Enhanced Summer Lights Program in District Eight through 2028, and accepted three donation agreements with LA Neighborhood Land Trust for landscaping improvements at Watts Towers Art Center. All measures passed unanimously or near-unanimously, with three council members absent throughout most votes.
notable: Nicole Chase appointed to Recreation and Parks Board; LA Public Library exempted from competitive contracting requirement for agreements
The Rescue Train granted parking space at East Valley Animal Services Center for spay and neuter operations
Enhanced Summer Lights Program funding extended through June 30, 2028 for Brotherhood Crusade in Council District Eight

la-primegov-18203.digest: The City Council passed 20 items with five members absent throughout the meeting. Major decisions included designating the Hollywood Center Motel neon sign as a historic-cultural monument; banning electric bicycles from equestrian and recreational trails; implementing 15 mph speed limits at 474 school-zone street segments citywide; approving funding for affordable housing accessibility improvements and bus service amendments; and establishing new parking restrictions and preferential parking districts in multiple council districts. Councilmember Jurado cast the only dissenting votes, opposing three separate oversize vehicle parking restrictions in Districts Eight, Fifteen, and Two.
notable: Council banned e-bikes from all equestrian, hiking, and recreational trails in Los Angeles.
Councilmember Jurado opposed three consecutive oversize vehicle parking restrictions (Districts 8, 15, and 2); all passed 11-1.
Council approved 15 mph speed limits near 214 schools across Los Angeles (474 street segments).

la-primegov-18172.digest: The City Council unanimously passed eight routine matters including extensions of the interim economic development manager's appointment, appointments to the Workforce Development Board, a review of lithium-ion battery and vape pen recycling programs, and adjustments to the winter shelter bed rate. The council also approved infrastructure and labor agreements related to traffic enforcement, the Glendale-Hyperion Bridge, and port and water department labor contracts through 2030. Four council members (Blumenfield, Nazarian, Padilla, and Park) were absent for most votes.
notable: Council member Park was absent for the final four votes; Blumenfield, Nazarian, and Padilla missed the first four items.
All eight motions passed with no dissenting votes recorded.
Labor agreement approved for Load Dispatchers Unit at LA Water and Power extending through September 2030.

la-primegov-18239.digest: The City Council approved seven routine matters including technology deployment for tracking bulky items, federal clean energy tax credit updates, WorkSource Center operator selection, new red flag fire warning protocols, rescission of brush clearance penalties for fire-damaged properties, and modifications to the Larchmont Village Business Improvement District. All items passed with 10–11 votes in favor and no recorded opposition, though four council members were absent for all votes and a fifth joined them for the final two items.

la-primegov-17721.digest: The City Council passed 18 routine items including easement dedications, emergency declarations for recent wildfires, and grant funding. A major action repealed the city's Gross Receipts Tax on virtually all businesses. Four separate wage and benefit amendments for airport and hotel workers were considered; one failed 10–5 with broad opposition, while two others passed with narrow splits (12–3 and 9–6) and one passed unanimously. Four council members were absent throughout the meeting.
notable: City repealed the Gross Receipts Tax on virtually all businesses.
Wage amendments for airport and hotel workers split the council: one failed 10–5; two others passed narrowly with 3–6 members voting no.
Council adopted the Mayor's emergency declarations from January 2025 wildfires and windstorms.

la-primegov-18267.digest: The City Council approved three measures related to the Port of Los Angeles and a local business improvement district, all passing 11-0 with four council members absent. The votes included modifying the Larchmont Village Property-Based Business Improvement District, approving a sublease between AltaSea and Blue Robotics at the port, and granting a permit to Taylored Transload and Waterfront Logistics. All three items passed without opposition from voting members.

la-primegov-18098.digest: Los Angeles City Council approved 27 items on May 19, 2026, including $40 million in educational facility bonds and $35 million in affordable housing bonds for a 78-unit project on Hoffman Street. The council voted to create a consolidated Community Investment Department and adopt a revised housing and homelessness framework, both opposed by Councilmembers Park and Rodriguez (and others); they also designated enforcement zones in Council District 6 against public sleeping and sitting, with four council members dissenting. The meeting covered routine business including special assessments, property liens, grant agreements, and community programs, all passing unanimously except for three split votes on homelessness/housing policy and employee wage rules.
notable: $75 million total authorized in bonds for education, affordable housing, and community projects
Creation of consolidated Community Investment Department and housing/homelessness framework both opposed by multiple council members
Four-member dissent on enforcement against sleeping/sitting in public spaces and on wage rules for airport/hotel workers

la-primegov-18285.digest: The City Council passed eight items with overwhelming support, including extending Abigail Marquez as Interim General Manager of the Department of Aging and approving amended contracts with three consulting firms (Impact Sciences, Architectural Resources Group, and Gartner) for various professional services. The council also advanced a General Plan Amendment and Zone Change for property at 16300 Foothill Boulevard and continued consideration of extending Alfred Mata's appointment as Interim City Engineer. Four council members were absent for most votes, with only two absent for the final item.
notable: Abigail Marquez's term extended as Interim General Manager of Department of Aging
Three amended consulting contracts approved (Impact Sciences, Architectural Resources Group, Gartner) with no dissent
Zone change initiated for 16300 Foothill Boulevard property

la-primegov-18241.digest: City Council passed the FY 2026-27 budget and approved Proposition K assessments; created a new consolidated Community Investment Department despite opposition from Padilla and Rodriguez; amended wage rules for airport and hotel workers with three dissenting votes (Hernandez, Jurado, Soto-Martínez); and approved major contracts including a second amendment to LAX baggage system work with Vanderlande Industries. Council also supported five state assembly bills on homelessness prevention and housing reform, funded several neighborhood infrastructure projects (Cypress Park pedestrian tunnel, Highland Park improvements, McCoy Avenue fencing), and withdrew a citizen ballot initiative to repeal business taxes.
notable: New Community Investment Department created by consolidating three agencies; Padilla and Rodriguez voted no.
Wage and benefit amendments for airport and hotel workers passed 11-3, with Hernandez, Jurado, and Soto-Martínez opposed.
FY 2026-27 budget adopted with support for five state homelessness and housing bills.

la-primegov-18292.digest: The City Council passed approximately 40 routine items including multiple property removals from the Rent Escrow Account Program (a housing enforcement tool), appointments of Gabrielle Amster as Department of Animal Services General Manager and Stewart Lozano to the Historical Monument Authority, and approvals for software installation at Van Nuys Council Chambers to meet state requirements. The council also funded pilot projects for the Fire Department and Police Department using Innovation Fund money, with Councilmember Hernandez opposing the Police Department project. Five council members were absent throughout most of the meeting, including McOsker, Raman, and Soto-Martínez.
notable: Councilmember Hernandez cast the only opposing vote on the Police Department's Booking Innovation Team pilot project; all other items passed unanimously among those present.
The city will hold a public hearing on job vacancies and recruitment before adopting the 2026-27 budget, which also faced one opposing vote from Councilmember Park.
Multiple properties were removed from the Rent Escrow Account Program, a city tool for enforcing housing standards on troubled rental properties.

la-primegov-18314.digest: The City Council passed six unanimously supported measures (14-0 with Councilmember Hernandez absent) to lease city properties to nonprofits for housing and services: Hope the Mission will operate interim housing sites at two Van Nuys Boulevard locations, West Valley Homes Yes will lease office space, and the city will amend office leases and renew a mail service contract. The council also designated the Chase Street and Van Nuys Boulevard intersection as Los Angeles Azules Square.

newyork-bos-2026-07-16.digest: The committee passed a requirement for the mayor to create a program that coordinates inspections and plan reviews across agencies, aiming to speed up how quickly new businesses can open. The measure passed unanimously with 7 votes in favor.
notable: Mayor must establish a new business inspection coordination program to reduce business opening timelines

newyork-bos-2026-07-16-Committee-on-Civi.digest: The Committee approved $10,000 in workforce stabilization payments for eligible school paraprofessionals in the 2026-2027 school year, funded by the Department of Education. The measure passed unanimously with seven votes in favor.
notable: Department of Education to distribute up to $10,000 per eligible paraprofessional starting next school year

newyork-bos-2026-07-14.digest: The Committee on Land Use designated Public School 15 Annex in Brooklyn as a landmark with unanimous approval (13 votes). A second item passed 13–0 but was not described in the minutes; residents should check the official record for details.
notable: Public School 15 Annex in Brooklyn now has landmark designation
One passed item has no subject stated in minutes—check official record

newyork-bos-2026-07-14-Subcommittee-on-Z.digest: The Zoning Subcommittee approved three rezoning actions: 164th Street in Queens, 198-208 Richmond Terrace in Staten Island, and a zoning text amendment for housing opportunity (with modifications). All votes were unanimous (9-0). One agenda item has no subject recorded in the minutes.
notable: All four items passed unanimously with 9 aye votes
Housing opportunity zoning text amendment approved with modifications and referred to City Planning Commission

newyork-bos-2026-07-14-Subcommittee-on-L.digest: The Landmarks Subcommittee approved the Church of Saint Mary landmark application in Manhattan with modifications and sent it to City Planning Commission for further review. Subcommittee member Feliz was absent from the vote.
notable: Church of Saint Mary landmark application approved with modifications (6-0, one absent)

pittsburgh-legistar-12034.digest: City Council approved six routine items including IT contracts totaling over $153,000, a software services increase of $520, coordination with LA28 for Olympic-related events at no cost, and a license for ALCOSAN to construct stormwater diversion structures. All passed unanimously 8–0 with Councilmember Deborah L. Gross absent.

pittsburgh-legistar-11951.digest: City Council unanimously approved 13 measures including a $500,000 boost to the Parks Trust Fund, restructuring of EMS positions (eliminating one District Division Chief, adding one Assistant Chief), and the conveyance of two city properties to qualified residents under affordability restrictions. Council also authorized the sale of 24 tax-acquired properties across multiple districts, granted Duquesne Light Company an easement for utility installation at no cost to the city, and realigned spending in federal grants to meet deadlines. A previous property sale was rescinded.
notable: Parks Trust Fund increased by $500,000 for 2026
EMS restructure: one District Division Chief position eliminated, one Assistant Chief position added
Two city properties conveyed to qualified residents with affordability restrictions

pittsburgh-legistar-11944.digest: City Council approved $134,831 for slope failure repairs and unanimously authorized over $3.6 million in additional spending on legal services, engineering contracts, and construction projects including the California Avenue Bridge and Pittsburgh City Steps. The council also approved repaving two downtown streets with asphalt and transferred vacant properties to the Pittsburgh Land Bank for redevelopment, while settling a vehicle damage claim for $7,496 and transferring a North Pacific Avenue property to resolve a legal case. All 13 items passed without dissent.
notable: $1.36 million contract increase for California Avenue Bridge engineering work, now totaling $2.2 million
$1.45 million contract for Pittsburgh City Steps construction inspection and administration
All votes unanimous (9 aye) with no opposition or abstentions

pittsburgh-legistar-11939.digest: City Council unanimously passed ten items including a $10,000 settlement for an employment matter with Justin Hickox, new restrictions on collecting immigration and citizenship information and using city assets for immigration enforcement, a $26,257.50 increase in geotechnical services funding for Police Firing Range improvements, and temporary parking enforcement rules for the NFL Draft. The council also extended the terms of the Acting Directors of the Office of Management and Budget and Department of Innovation & Performance each by 90 days, accepted dedication of two city parcels in the 9th Ward at no cost, and directed a report on city surveillance technologies.
notable: All ten motions passed unanimously or near-unanimously (Planning Commission deadline extension passed 8-aye)
New city ordinance limits police use of immigration enforcement and city asset use for immigration-related purposes
$26,257.50 increase in Police Firing Range engineering and geotechnical services funding

pittsburgh-legistar-11931.digest: City Council approved $20,000 for police training, hired an attorney for the Citizen Police Review Board at up to $45,000 annually, and authorized $178,939 in additional funding for Penn Avenue reconstruction oversight. The council also used eminent domain to acquire easements for Andover Terrace repairs in the Fifth Ward (budgeting $35,000), with Councilmember Lavelle abstaining, and approved over $300,000 in contracts for various city services including snow removal and landscape management. All 16 items passed without opposition.
notable: Council member R. Daniel Lavelle abstained on the eminent domain vote for Andover Terrace easement acquisition ($35,000), while eight voted in favor.
Penn Avenue Phase 2 Reconstruction Project received $178,939 in additional engineering oversight funding.
Council designated properties in Upper Lawrenceville as permanent open space for the Allegheny River Greenway program.

pittsburgh-legistar-11924.digest: City Council unanimously approved ten routine matters: payments for court reporting ($5,556), vehicle repairs ($6,348), and police chief association dues ($7,500); a new code chapter on right-of-way accessibility; a no-cost natural gas agreement for Riverview Park; environmental restrictions on Spring Hill Park; eminent domain authority for Crafton Borough; a $152,000 three-year police recruitment consulting contract; a $25,000 greenways signage grant; and reallocation of $55,693 in federal COVID relief funds to two food banks. All votes were 9-0 with no dissent.
notable: Largest expenditure: $151,980 over three years to Industrial/Organization Solutions for police recruit exam consulting
$55,693 in CARES Act funds redirected to food banks
Crafton Borough granted eminent domain authority over Pittsburgh property

pittsburgh-legistar-11915.digest: City Council unanimously approved $300,000 in spending over three years for group violence intervention technology through Operation Better Block, plus accepted over $805,500 in state grant funds to replace traffic signals at Fifth and an unnamed street. The council also authorized free warehouse space from The Buncher Company for police training and event space, transferred a city property to the Pittsburgh Land Bank at no cost, and approved sewage facility plan updates for three development projects. Routine expenses included $21,824 for U.S. Conference of Mayors membership, $52,962 for Pennsylvania Municipal League subscription, and $19,626 for National League of Cities membership.
notable: Council approved $300,000 investment in Operation Better Block violence intervention program over three years
Received $805,533 state grant for traffic signal replacement (location incomplete in record)
All 15 items passed unanimously with no dissent or failed motions

pittsburgh-legistar-11900.digest: City Council approved appointments of five people to the Planning Commission, along with appointments to several boards including the Equipment Leasing Authority and Pittsburgh Water. The body also authorized $1 million in additional spending for the Equipment Leasing Authority's vehicle and equipment purchases (now totaling $28.2 million), granted Three Rivers Waterkeeper a five-year contract to manage Island Marina for $1 annually, and approved $37,500 to develop historic district design guidelines for Mexican War Streets and Manchester neighborhoods. Councilmember Khari Mosley was absent; two appointees to the Planning Commission—David Vatz and Gerardo Interiano—faced dissenting votes.
notable: Equipment Leasing Authority spending increased by $1 million to $28.2 million total.
David Vatz's Planning Commission appointment passed 6–2, with Deborah Gross and Barbara Warwick opposed.
Three Rivers Waterkeeper granted five-year Island Marina management contract for $1 annually with renewal option.

pittsburgh-legistar-11893.digest: City Council unanimously approved twelve items totaling over $440,000 in spending and settlements, including $2,650 to settle an EMS vehicle damage claim, $62,000 to settle federal litigation, and $86,733.76 to increase the Sylvan Avenue Multimodal Path Project contract. The council also redirected $42,000 between two Lawrenceville organizations for public service grants, contracted with Carnegie Mellon University for up to $200,000 over three years for a Safe Streets project, and created a new housing data dashboard ordinance. All votes were unanimous with no dissent.
notable: $62,000 federal court settlement with Naeem Martinez and Max Petrunya, PC was the largest single payment approved.
Sylvan Avenue Multimodal Path Project budget increased by $86,733.76, bringing total contract to $1.7 million.
$42,000 in neighborhood funding redirected from Lawrenceville United to Lawrenceville Corporation for public service grants.

pittsburgh-legistar-11882.digest: City Council unanimously approved $485,400 total for labor dispute legal services from Fisher Phillips, LLP, and authorized $56.02 million in general obligation bonds for city projects. Major spending increases include $1.27 million more for design management services (CDR Maguire), $620,000 additional reimbursement for the Smithfield Street reconstruction, and expansion of the Landforce urban forestry contract to $465,500 over four years. The council also accepted federal and state grants totaling over $1 million for fire/EMS equipment and training, settled litigation with Benjamin Mayhew for $60,000, and adopted new zoning regulations for waste transfer stations.
notable: $56.02 million in general obligation bonds authorized for city projects
Fisher Phillips labor dispute contract nearly quintupled from $60,000 to $485,400 over two years
Smithfield Street reconstruction state reimbursement increased by $620,000 (80% reimbursed); Planning Commission deadline extended on zoning matter (8 aye, one opposed or abstained)

pittsburgh-legistar-11873.digest: City Council approved a $10,000 settlement for an employment matter (with Councilwoman Warwick abstaining) and authorized over $45,000 in preliminary engineering agreements for bridge projects with railroad companies, all fully reimbursable. The council also accepted multiple state grants for transportation and safety projects on Liberty Avenue, Main Street, Lincoln Avenue, and related intersections, and executed three cooperation agreements with the School District and housing development corporations for the Steelworkers Tower, Lynn Williams Apartments, and I.W. Abel Building. Additionally, publicly-owned properties in the 10th Ward were transferred to the Pittsburgh Land Bank at no cost. Councilwoman Deborah L. Gross was absent from the meeting.
notable: Settlement of $10,000 approved for employment matter with Barbara Warwick abstaining
Over $45,000 authorized for bridge preliminary engineering with railroad companies (fully reimbursable)
Multiple state grants accepted and 10th Ward properties transferred to Pittsburgh Land Bank

pittsburgh-legistar-11867.digest: City Council unanimously approved $19 million in grants and donations for ambulances, emergency medical vehicles, and snow removal equipment, along with $27.2 million in equipment leasing transfers. The council also reduced water service reappropriations by $1 million, changed property tax discount dates, approved a street vacation, and extended the Acting Director of Mobility's term through May. All 15 items passed with either 8 or 9 votes in favor and no recorded dissent.
notable: $10 million donation from UPMC for EMS vehicles and equipment approved
Unanimous passage of all 15 motions with no failed or split votes
$19 million in total grants secured for ambulances, emergency equipment, and snow removal

pittsburgh-legistar-11860.digest: City Council approved six measures focused on transportation infrastructure and recreation funding. The council authorized reimbursements totaling millions of dollars from the Pennsylvania Department of Transportation for three separate road projects—East Liberty-Negley Avenue, Brownsville Road Safety Improvements, and the Herron Avenue Bridge—along with a preliminary engineering agreement for the bridge work. A separate measure authorized the Mayor and Parks Director to contract with performers and instructors for recreational programs, and the city adopted a sewage plan revision for 824 James Street. All items passed unanimously or near-unanimously with no reported dissent.
notable: $2.57 million PennDOT reimbursement approved for East Liberty-Negley Avenue construction
Council increased city costs for Herron Avenue Bridge project through amended reimbursement agreement
Parks and Recreation authorized to contract with performers and instructors for recreational programs (8 aye, 1 absent)

pittsburgh-legistar-11852.digest: City Council approved $1.39 million in state transportation grants for intersection upgrades and authorized land acquisition by eminent domain for two unspecified projects (Cal and Wes, likely abbreviations). The council also greenlit $19,580 in preliminary engineering for the Elizabeth Street Bridge rehabilitation with CSX Transportation, accepted a $50,000 county grant for the Warrington Recreation Center, and approved several smaller items including zoning rules for the North Shore Entertainment District and a job-analysis contract for police positions. All eight present council members voted unanimously; Anthony Coghill was absent.
notable: $1.39M state grant approved for intersection improvements
City authorized to use eminent domain for two transportation projects if needed
$50,000 county grant secured for Warrington Recreation Center

pittsburgh-legistar-11825.digest: City Council approved a $7 million fund reallocation to cover Pittsburgh Water costs in 2026, paused the Stop the Violence Fund for one year (over Councilmember Khari Mosley's objection), and contracted with five community organizations for violence prevention services totaling $2.6 million over three years. The council also authorized seizure of the 100-unit Homewood House apartment building to preserve it as affordable housing, transferred multiple vacant city lots to the Pittsburgh Land Bank, and approved various service contracts and policy updates.
notable: $7 million reallocated from 2025 to 2026 for Pittsburgh Water costs
Stop the Violence Fund paused in 2026 with one dissenting vote (Mosley opposed)
City to seize Homewood House apartment building (100 units) and preserve as affordable housing

pittsburgh-legistar-11813.digest: City Council approved $6 million over two years for The Center that CARES violence intervention services, granted $44,600 in state funding for South Side Trail repaving, and applied for $2.8 million in additional state grants for parks, bikeshare expansion, and fire equipment. The council also terminated two planning consultant contracts with three dissenting votes (Lavelle, Mosley, Strassburger opposed), settled three lawsuits for $15,000 total, and authorized multiple property transactions and five-year no-cost agreements with nonprofits for recreation center use.
notable: Council split 6-3 on terminating contracts with Sasaki & Common Cause Consultants and HR&A Advisors; Lavelle, Mosley, and Strassburger all opposed.
Approved $6 million commitment to The Center that CARES for violence intervention and social services over two years.
Designated Police Station 3 on Penn Avenue as historic site, with 2 opposing votes (Gross, Warwick).

pittsburgh-legistar-11806.digest: City Council passed its complete 2026 budget and financial framework, including the real estate tax rate, fee schedule, and $1.675 million in payments to the Urban Redevelopment Authority. Councilmember Theresa Kail-Smith opposed the tax rate, fee schedule, and employee compensation measures, while Councilmember Bobby Wilson also voted against the tax rate. Councilmember Anthony Coghill was absent for all votes.
notable: Real estate tax rate for 2026 passed 6-2 despite opposition from Kail-Smith and Wilson
Theresa Kail-Smith voted no on three budget items (tax rate, fee schedule, employee compensation)
$1.675 million authorized for Urban Redevelopment Authority to manage city development projects

pittsburgh-legistar-11804.digest: Council unanimously approved 26 items in one meeting, spending roughly $9.4 million across settlements, PennDOT reimbursements, bridge work, and service contracts, while one member absent. Major outlays: $499,999 to settle Broad Street remediation litigation, $5.3 million added to a PennDOT agreement (bringing it to $63.7 million total), $1.2 million increase for 28th Street Bridge design, and $150,000 more for vehicle leasing. Council also transferred city property at 5472 Broad Street, authorized eminent domain for Liberty Avenue sidewalk work, and made the Mobility Enhancement District parking program permanent. Councilmember Robert Charland opposed designating Westinghouse Park as a historic site; all other votes were 8–0 with Barbara Warwick absent.
notable: $9.4 million in major spending approved unanimously, including $499,999 Broad Street litigation settlement and $5.3 million added to one PennDOT reimbursement agreement.
Robert Charland sole opposition to Westinghouse Park historic designation (7–1); all other 26 items passed 8–0.
Council committed to $3.6+ million in additional bridge and transit infrastructure funding across multiple PennDOT agreements.

pittsburgh-legistar-11792.digest: Council unanimously approved 40+ spending and contract authorizations totaling tens of millions of dollars, with the largest being a $6.9 million salt storage facility construction contract, a $400,000 increase to fleet management (raising it to $79.2 million over six years), and $2.4 million added to the payroll system contract. The council also approved three major bridge projects receiving combined $4.65 million in additional state reimbursement funding at no city cost, and settled four liability claims totaling $62,033. All votes passed 9–0 with no dissent or failed motions.
notable: $6.9M salt storage facility construction contract and $400K fleet management increase are the two largest spending hikes.
All 40+ items passed unanimously; no split votes, failed motions, or dissent recorded.
Three bridge projects (Calera, Corley, California Ave) received combined $4.65M in state reimbursement with no local cost impact.

pittsburgh-legistar-11779.digest: City Council approved eleven spending measures totaling over $1.4 million, with Councilmember Deborah L. Gross absent and Theresa Kail-Smith voting no on a $75,000 community reinvestment analysis payment. Major approvals included a $900,000 expansion of legal defense contracts for workers' compensation claims, $75,000 more for communications services, and $20,000 additional legal fees for a construction project, plus a $15,000 grant for firefighting foam and various vehicle/equipment leasing authorizations.
notable: Largest single commitment: $900,000 increase to legal defense contract for workers' comp claims over three years, raising total to $3.6 million
Split vote: Theresa Kail-Smith opposed $75,000 payment to Pittsburgh Community Reinvestment Group for community reinvestment analysis
Communications contract extended 6 months with $75,000 increase to total $703,976

pittsburgh-legistar-11770.digest: City Council unanimously approved the sale of tax-foreclosed properties, a $674,193 engineering contract for the Bloomfield Bridge Project, and a $240,000 subscription for weather-hazard planning data. Council also settled a $5,090 property damage claim and approved a $190,000 increase for safety candidate assessments. Two council members (Gross and Warwick) opposed transferring a leased water authority property to the city for $1.00, which passed 7–2.
notable: Bloomfield Bridge engineering contract approved at $674,192.87, fully reimbursable.
Two dissenting votes on water authority property transfer: Deborah L. Gross and Barbara Warwick opposed.
City spending $240,000 for weather-risk database to plan for climate-related hazards across departments.

pittsburgh-legistar-11759.digest: City Council passed 13 measures with one absent member, approving $2.4 million in county funding for children's programs, $400,000 for Carnegie Library Squirrel Hill HVAC/roofing repairs, $300,000 for playground upgrades, and $571,250 for hazardous waste recycling. The council also reformed the Youth Commission structure, updated zoning and vending regulations, transferred city-owned lots to developers and the Land Bank at no cost, and adjusted urban redevelopment project parcels in the 5th Ward. All votes were unanimous among the eight present members.
notable: $2.4 million two-year agreement with Allegheny County for children, youth, and family programs passed unanimously
City transferred three lots in Council District 3 to Bridgeside Point 1 LLC and two lots in Council District 6 to Pittsburgh Land Bank at no cost
Council member Theresa Kail-Smith was absent from the entire meeting

pittsburgh-legistar-11747.digest: City Council approved roughly $31.5 million in major infrastructure spending, including nearly $9.6 million for Penn Avenue Phase 2 construction (reimbursable at 80%) and $705,000 in additional design costs for the Pittsburgh City Steps project, along with $5.8 million for community development block grants. The council also accepted $50,000 in county hazardous materials funding, authorized free access to state police traffic software for public safety, and approved civil rights code updates with one abstention from Theresa Kail-Smith. All motions passed unanimously or with minimal dissent.
notable: Penn Avenue Phase 2 construction contract with Gulisek LLC authorized at $9.5M (80% reimbursable)
Civil rights code clarification passed 8-0-1, with Theresa Kail-Smith abstaining
Total major infrastructure approvals exceeded $31 million across multiple projects

pittsburgh-legistar-11742.digest: City Council unanimously passed 23 items totaling over $6.5 million in spending and commitments, dominated by housing and social services. The council redirected $505,769 from sports facilities to park reconstruction, committed up to $2.5 million in local matching funds for federal air quality grants on two major avenue projects, and transferred nine city-owned properties to the Pittsburgh Land Bank for redevelopment. Significant allocations went to homelessness services ($1.18 million in emergency shelter funds), HIV/AIDS housing support ($1.4 million), and community development programs, while also canceling three prior property sales and authorizing legal settlements and consulting contracts.
notable: Council committed $2.5M+ in bond funds as local match for federal air quality grants on East Warrington and Murray Avenue projects.
Nine city-owned properties transferred to Pittsburgh Land Bank to support affordable housing and land reuse.
All 23 items passed unanimously (9-0) with no dissenting votes or failed motions.

pittsburgh-legistar-11738.digest: City Council passed nine measures with unanimous support from the seven present members (two absent). Key actions include acquiring private property on Herndon Street via eminent domain for up to $6,000, authorizing a $204,750 payment plan with Duquesne Light for overdue electricity bills, rezoning two parcels to mixed-use development, and awarding an additional $500,000 to the Brighton Heights Healthy Active Living Center. The council also approved a $2,000 budget reallocation to the City Clerk's office and maintenance of the pedestrian wayfinding project at no city cost.
notable: Council will pay overdue electricity bills to Duquesne Light via $204,750 one-year agreement
Brighton Heights Healthy Active Living Center grant increased by $500,000 to $2.73 million total
Two residential parcels rezoned to urban center mixed-use; one Herndon Street property authorized for eminent domain acquisition

princewilliam-bos-2026-07-14.digest: The Board of County Supervisors blocked a utility company's request to use county property for two routes of a 230-kilovolt power transmission line between Nokesville and Bristow. The board also voted to explore a trap-neuter-vaccinate-release program for feral cats, with Supervisor Vega dissenting. A joint meeting with the Prince William County School Board was scheduled for September 23, 2026.
notable: Board denied utility easement access for Routes 5 and 12 of the Nokesville-Bristow 230kV transmission line.
Feral cat management program approved 7–1, with Supervisor Vega opposing.
Joint meeting with School Board scheduled for September 23, 2026 at 6 p.m.
